note:
首先，我应该说，我理解谷歌在这里的意图，即统一其套件中各种应用程序的视觉语言。这一点可能很重要，特别是对于谷歌这样的公司来说，它放弃了应用程序、服务、设计语言和其他东西，比如从下沉的热气球上压载(实际上，这是一个非常恰当的比较)。
这些年来，我们已经看到了如此之多的谷歌图标语言，以至于很难让自己去关心新的语言。套用孙子的话说，如果你在河边等待足够长的时间，你最喜欢的谷歌产品的身体就会漂浮过去。最好不要依恋。
但有时他们会做一些如此愚蠢的事情，以至于任何关心公司的人都有责任当面为公司辩护，告诉他们他们搞砸了；我上一次足够在意的是谷歌阅读器(Google Reader)。由于我和其他一亿人将不得不整天盯着这些丑陋的新图标，直到他们退休，也许制造一点噪音会加速这一时间表。
抱歉，如果我让我自己在这里散文一点，但我认为这是对这些几乎无一例外的不明智的重新设计总是伴随着没完没了的设计故事的一种解药。我将讨论这些图标是如何出错的，主要有三个方面：颜色、形状和品牌。
颜色是你最先注意到的东西之一，即使在你的周边视觉中，你也可以很容易地识别颜色。因此，拥有鲜明的颜色对于排字和设计在很多方面都很重要。你认为为什么公司会对这些不同深浅的蓝色如此着迷呢？
这就是为什么最受欢迎的谷歌应用程序的图标如此容易区分的部分原因。Gmail的红色可以追溯到十多年前，而Calendar的蓝色也相当古老。Meet的青绿色可能就应该保持绿色，就像它的前身Hangout一样，但它至少有些不同。同样的保留(还记得保留吗？)。还有其他几个不太出名的演员。更重要的是，它们是坚固的-除了一些颜色更好的，比如地图，在它的图标被暗杀之前。
新图标的颜色有两个问题。首先，它们没有真正的颜色。他们都有所有的颜色，这使得他们很难一目了然地区分开来。请记住，您永远不会看到上图中的这么大。更常见的情况是，它们的大小更多是这样的：
也许更小。而且从来没有那么近过。我不知道你是怎么想的，但是当我不直视它们的时候，我就分不清它们了。你到底在找什么？它们都有各种颜色，甚至不同的顺序或方向-你看到一些是红的，黄的，绿的，蓝的，还有一个是红的，黄的，蓝的，绿的吗？三个(使用Gmail)顺时针，两个逆时针。听起来并不重要，但你的眼睛会注意到这样的东西，但可能就足够让你更困惑了。如果它们都从左上角的红色开始，或者别的什么，然后循环通过，也许这些会更好。他们不会随机化谷歌主徽标中的颜色顺序，对吗？归根结底，这些小斑点就像玩具或嘎吱作响的糖果包装纸。充其量只是格子呢，那是松弛的领地。
起初，我认为红色的小三角形标签是一个很好的视觉指示器，但不知何故，它们也搞砸了这一点。每个图标都应该将选项卡放在不同的角落，但“日历”和“驱动器”都在右下角。我想它们是不同种类的三角形--这是三角学的免费赠品。
您还会注意到图标有一种不平衡的权重。这是因为在浅色的背景下，不同的颜色有不同的视觉突显。更深的颜色在白色背景下比黄色或一点点红色更突出，使得图标看起来有很重的“L”方面，在Gmail和Calendar中位于左侧，在Drive and Meet中位于左下角，在Docs中位于右下角。但在不活动的选项卡中，浅色会更加突出，而那些L会看起来在另一边。
这是对形状问题的一个很好的切入，因为这些图标的感知形状将根据背景的不同而改变。最初的图标通过其独特的实心形状解决了这个问题，而且背景并没有真正渗漏出来。你必须非常小心你的设计中的透明部分--正面和负面的空间等等。如果你把你的徽标的任何部分放到后台，你就会对用户选择的任何UI或主题心血来潮。这些徽标中间有个洞，看着深灰色的非活动标签，会不会很好看？或者这个洞是用白色填充的，在深色背景上是正空间，在白色背景上是负空间吗？
不管怎么说，这些图标的问题是它们的形状不好。它们都是空心的，其中四个是矩形的，如果你把Gmail的负空格包括在内(我们确实是这样做的--谷歌教我们这样做)。一个容器的总体形状是完美的，但是一眼看上去有四个基本上就是角O。你想要高的O，尖的还是两个颜色略有不同的正方形O中的一个呢？在远处，谁能说得清呢？如果你仔细观察，它们现在才会和它们应该做的事情相似。
现在我想起来了，那些形状真的很像Office和Bing，不是吗？不太好！
当我们谈到这一点时，与粗大的边框相比，日历空地上的薄型显得相当贫乏，对吧？也许他们应该用粗体。
最后，重叠的颜色会带来麻烦。首先，它让Drive的标志看起来像是一个生物危害的象征。但它增加了很大的复杂性，这在小范围内是很难做到的。可以肯定的是，最初的Drive标志有三种颜色，还有一个小阴影，这样你就会看到它是一个莫比乌斯条纹，寓意无限，而不仅仅是一个三角形(三角形也不见了-所以为什么要保留这个三角形呢？)-但颜色相互映衬：蓝色和黄色代表绿色，两个原色和次要颜色。
新的三种颜色都有三种原色，一种是二级的，两种是三级的(如果你把黑暗算作一种颜色的话)。它们无助于这些形状以任何可识别的方式存在。你在看它们吗？这看起来不太对劲。它们有点折叠，但如何折叠呢？这些带子是用捻线做的吗？我不这样认为。这些形状不是东西-它们只是安排，是对曾经的东西的暗示，走得太远了一步。
谷歌对把价值扔进垃圾桶并不陌生。但你会认为，有时他们会意识到他们有好的事情要做。Gmail徽标是一件好事。我不得不说，几年前当他们换成圆形图标时，我更喜欢老的棱角的，但我越来越喜欢它了。信封上自然的“M”字形得到了很好的强调，红白相间的颜色非常容易辨认和阅读--这是一种让你长时间坚持的标志。要不就不要了！
这里的问题是，十多年来，基本上一直作为自己的、完全不可战胜的品牌运营的Gmail(这在科技领域已经是亿万年了，更不用说科技标识了)，已经与其他不那么受信任或不那么广泛使用的服务平起平坐了。
现在Gmail只是一片彩虹形状的海洋中的另一个彩虹形状，它告诉用户“这项服务对我们来说并不特殊。这么长时间以来，这并不是一项为你工作得很好的服务。这只是互联网巨头手中的一根手指。而现在，你每次看到其中一个，都会想着另一个。“。
所有其他的小色轮都是一样的：你永远不会忘记，它们都是同一装置的一部分，它知道你搜索的一切，你访问的每个网站，以及现在你在工作中做的一切。哦，他们对此很有礼貌。但别搞错了，同质化的品牌(尽管其颜色各异)是一场品牌危机的前奏，在这场危机中，你不再只是Gmail的用户，你整天、每天都在谷歌的房子里。
谷歌副总裁哈维尔·索特罗(Javier Soltero)告诉“快公司”(Fast Company)：“这是我们从定义我们的产品结构和角色的时刻开始的，这些产品是别人在一个非常不同的时代发明的。”
信息很明确：抛弃那些建立你信任的旧东西；用新东西加入那些利用你信任的东西。
note_en:
First I should say that I understand Google’s intent here, to unify the visual language of the various apps in its suite. That can be important, especially with a company like Google, which abandons apps, services, design languages, and other things like ballast out of a sinking hot air balloon (a remarkably apt comparison, in fact).
 We’ve seen so many Google icon languages over the years that it’s hard to bring oneself to care about new ones. To paraphrase Sun Tzu, if you wait long enough by the river, the bodies of your favorite Google products will float by. Better not to get attached.
  But sometimes they do something so senseless that it is incumbent upon anyone who cares at all to throw the company’s justification in its face and tell them they blew it; The last time I cared enough was with Google Reader. Since I and a hundred million other people will have to stare at these ugly new icons all day until they retire them, maybe making a little noise will accelerate that timeline a bit.
  Sorry if I let myself prose a bit here, but I consider it an antidote to the endless design stories these almost  without exception  ill-advised  redesigns always come with. I’ll limit discussion of how these icons go wrong to three general ways: color, shape, and brand.
  Color is one of the first things you notice about something, and you can recognize colors easily even in your peripheral vision. So having a distinct color is important to type and design in lots of ways. Why do you think companies go so crazy about all those different shades of blue?
 That’s part of why the icons of the most popular Google apps are so easily distinguished. Gmail’s red color goes back a decade and more, and Calendar’s blue is pretty old as well. The teal of Meet probably should have just stayed green, like its predecessor Hangouts, but it’s at least somewhat distinct. Likewise Keep (remember Keep?) and a handful of other lesser actors. More importantly, they’re solid — except for a few that were better for their colors, like Maps, before  its icon got assassinated.
 There are two problems with the colors of the new icons. First is that they don’t really have colors. They  all have  all the colors, which just right off the bat makes it harder to tell them apart at a glance. Remember, you’re never going to see this big like in the image above. More often they’ll be more this size:
 Maybe even smaller. And never that close. I don’t know about you, but I can’t tell them apart when I’m not looking directly at them. What exactly are you looking for? They all have every color, and not even in the same order or direction — you see how some are red, yellow, green, blue and one is red, yellow, blue, green? Three (with Gmail) clockwise and two anti-clockwise, too. Sounds unimportant but your eye picks up on stuff like that, but maybe just enough that you’re  more confused. Maybe these would have been better if they all started with red in the top left or something, and cycled through. They don’t randomize the order of the colors in the main Google logo, right? Ultimately these little blobs just resemble toys or crunched up candy wrappers. At best it’s plaid, and that’s Slack territory.
 At first I thought the little red triangular tabs were a nice visual indicator, but somehow they messed that up too. Each icon should have the tab in a different corner, but Calendar and Drive both have it on the bottom right. They’re different kinds of triangles, I suppose — that’s a freebie from trigonometry.
 You’ll also notice that the icons have a sort of lopsided weight. That’s because against a light background, different colors have different visual salience. Darker colors pop more against a white background than yellow or the tiny bit of red, making the icons seem to have heavy “L” aspects to them, on the left in Gmail and Calendar, bottom left in Drive and Meet, bottom right in Docs. But in an inactive tab, the light color will be more salient, and those L’s will seem to be on the other sides.
  This is a good segue into the shape problems, because the perceived shape of these icons will change depending on the background. The original icons solved this by having a solid shape unique to them, and the background didn’t really leak through. You have to be real careful about transparent parts of your design — positive and negative space and all that. If you surrender any part of your logo to the background, you’re at the whim of whatever UI or theme the user has chosen. Will these logos look good with a hole in the middle looking onto a dark grey inactive tab? Or will the hole be filled in with white, making it positive space when on a dark background and negative when on white?
 Anyhow the issue with these icons is that their shapes are bad. They’re all hollow, and four of them are rectangular if you include Gmail’s negative space (and we do — Google taught us to). The general shape of a container is a perfectly good one, but at a glance four of them are basically just angular O’s. Do you want the tallish O, the pointy one, or one of the two square O’s with slightly different color patterns? At a distance, who can tell? They only now resemble the thing they’re supposed do if you look really closely.
 Now that I think of it, those shapes really scream Office and Bing too, don’t they? Not great!
 While we’re at it, the thin type in the Calendar’s open space is pretty anemic compared with the big thick border, right? Maybe they should have gone with bold.
 And last, the overlapping colors make for trouble. For one thing it makes the Drive logo look like a biohazard symbol. But it adds a lot of complexity that’s hard to follow at a small scale. The original Drive logo had three colors, to be sure, and a little drop shadow so you’d see it was a Moebius strip implying infinity and not just a triangle (that’s gone too — so why keep the triangle?) — but the colors set each other off: Blue and yellow make green, two primaries and their secondary.
 The new ones have all three primaries, one secondary, and two tertiary (if you count darkness as a color). They don’t help the shapes exist in any identifiable way. Are you looking through them? That doesn’t seem right. They kind of fold, but how? Are the strips these are made of twisting? I don’t think so. The shapes aren’t things — they’re just arrangements, suggestions of the things they once were, removed one step too far.
  Google’s no stranger to throwing value in the trash. But you’d think that sometimes they’d recognize when they have a good thing going. The Gmail logo was a good thing. I have to say I preferred the old angular one when they switched to the rounded icon some years back, but it’s grown on me. The natural “M” shape of a the envelope is emphasized so well, and the red-and-white color is so instantly recognizable and readable — this is the kind of logo you hold onto for a long, long time. Or not!
 The problem here is that now Gmail, which has essentially operated as its own, completely invincible brand for more than a decade (which is eons in tech, let alone tech logos), has been put on equal footing with other services that aren’t as trusted or as widely used.
  Now Gmail is just another rainbow shape in a sea of very similar rainbow shapes, which tells the user “this service isn’t special to us. This is not the service that has worked so well for you, for so long. This is just one finger on the hand of an internet giant. And now you can never see one without thinking of the other.”
 Same for all the rest of these little color wheels: You’ll never forget that they’re all part of the same apparatus that knows everything you search for, every site you visit, and now, everything you do at work. Oh, they’re very polite about it. But make no mistake, the homogeneous branding (for all its color heterogeneity) is the prelude to a brand crunch in which you are no longer just a Gmail user, you’re in Google’s house, all day, every day.
 “This is the moment in which we break free from defining the structure and the role of our offerings in terms that were invented by somebody else in a very different era,” Google VP Javier Soltero  told Fast Company.
 The message is clear: Out with the old — the things that built your trust; and in with the new — the things that capitalize on your trust.
title: 谷歌的新徽标很糟糕

note:
Facebook今天不仅禁止Qanon的暴力内容，还禁止“任何代表Qanon的Facebook页面、群组和Instagram账户”，这是这家社交巨头在日益激烈的选举之前为清理其平台而升级的举措。
QAnon是一套相互交织的支持特朗普的阴谋论，已经在美国选民中扎根。它的更极端的追随者在以暴力和危险的方式行事后，被指控犯有恐怖主义罪，这是由于他们坚持不寻常且往往前后不一的信仰体系。BuzzFeed新闻最近决定将QAnon称为“集体错觉”，这是对该理论空洞、愚蠢和危险信念的另一个恰当的称呼。
Facebook控制Qanon的努力是有帮助的，但可能为时已晚。在过去的一年里，QAnon从一个边缘阴谋论膨胀为一个令人震惊的主流政治信仰体系-一个甚至有自己的国会候选人的体系。这种增长是由社交网络推动的，社交网络天生就是为了让志同道合的人相互联系而设计的，这一功能一次又一次地被发现，用来传播错误信息，并引导用户走向越来越激进的信念。
今年7月，Twitter以担心“线下伤害”为由，对Qanon采取了自己的行动。该公司降低了QAnon内容的排名，将其从热门页面和算法建议中删除。Twitter的政策变化，就像Facebook之前的政策一样，没有完全禁止这些内容，但确实采取了行动来遏制其传播。
其他公司，如Alphabet的YouTube产品，也受到了外部观察人士的类似指责。(YouTube表示，它修改了算法，以便更好地过滤掉内容组合中较暗的部分，但该实验的结果远未得出结论。)。
Facebook和Twitter等社交平台在选举前面对故意造假的政府后，也对自己的规则进行了修改。同一政府在选举前散布关于投票安全和病毒的谎言和虚假信息，这种病毒已导致20多万美国人死亡。这两个人为限制这两种特别危险的错误信息所做的工作是值得的，但通过采取被动姿态而不是主动姿态，这些政策选择中的大多数也来得太晚，无法控制危险内容的病毒式传播。
Facebook的新规定将于今天生效，该公司在一份新闻稿中表示，现在正在“相应地删除内容”，但清除Qanon的努力“需要时间”。
是什么推动了Facebook的变化？根据该公司的说法，在猛烈抨击QAnon暴力材料后，它看到了“其他QAnon内容与现实世界不同形式的伤害有关，包括最近声称西海岸野火是由某些团体引发的。”在俄勒冈州，森林大火最近肆虐，Facebook平台上的错误信息导致该州居民被误导，他们认为Antifa-这个术语适用于那些反对法西斯主义的人，认为他们是在焚烧该州，设置非法路障。
目前尚不清楚Facebook在清除QAnon相关内容方面会有多大的成效，但我们将对此进行跟踪。
note_en:
Today’s move by Facebook to not only ban violent QAnon content but “any Facebook Pages, Groups and Instagram accounts representing QAnon” is an escalation by the social giant to clean its platform ahead of an increasingly contentious election.
 QAnon is a sprawling set of interwoven pro-Trump conspiracy theories that has taken root inside swaths of the American electorate. Its more extreme adherents have  been charged with terrorism after acting out in violent and dangerous ways, spurred on by their adherence to the unusual and often incoherent belief system. BuzzFeed News recently  decided to call QAnon a “collective delusion,” another apt title for the theory’s inane, fatuous and dangerous beliefs.
  Facebook’s effort to rein in QAnon is helpful, but likely too late. Over the course of the last year, QAnon swelled from a fringe conspiracy theory into a shockingly mainstream political belief system — one that even  has its own Congressional candidates. That growth was powered by social networks inherently designed to connect like-minded people to one another, a feature that has been found time and time again to spread misinformation and usher users toward increasingly radical beliefs.
  In July,  Twitter took action of its own against QAnon, citing concerns about “offline harm.” The company downranked QAnon content, removing it from trending pages and algorithmic suggestions. Twitter’s policy change, like Facebook’s previous one, stopped short of banning the content outright but did move to contain its spread.
 Other companies, like Alphabet’s YouTube product, have come under similar censure by external observers. (YouTube says it  reworked its algorithm to better filter out the darker shores of its content mix, but the results of that experiment are far from conclusive.)
  Social platforms like Facebook and Twitter have also made changes to their rules after being confronted with a  willfully mendacious administration ahead of an election, about which the same administration has propagated lies and disinformation about voting security and the virus that has killed more than 200,000 Americans. The pairs’ work to limit those two particularly risky strains of misinformation is worthy, but by taking a reactive posture instead of a proactive one most of those policy choices have also come too late to control the viral spread of dangerous content.
 Facebook’s new rule comes into force today, with the company saying in a release that it is now “removing content accordingly,” but that the effort to purge QAnon “will take time.”
 What drove the change at Facebook? According to the company, after it yanked violent QAnon material, it saw “other QAnon content tied to different forms of real world harm, including recent claims that the west coast wildfires were started by certain groups.” In Oregon, where forest fires recently raged, misinformation on the Facebook platform led to misinformed state residents who believed that antifa — a term applied to those opposed to fascism as an unironic pejorative — were torching the state,  set up illegal roadblocks.
 How effective Facebook will be at clearing QAnon-related content from its various platforms is not clear today, but will be something that we’ll track.
title: Facebook表示将在其平台上禁止QAnon
title_en: Facebook says it will ban QAnon across its platforms

note:
对于初创企业来说，最相关的点是该委员会为解决大型科技公司问题提出的潜在解决方案，这些解决方案主要归结为，当小公司声称规模较大的竞争对手正在行使垄断优势时，它们将获得怀疑的好处-并阻止未来的那种收购，这些收购使这些公司能够达到它们目前在所选市场上无懈可击的地位。
委员会断言，在它们的核心业务领域：搜索、电子商务、社交网络和移动开发平台和应用程序，每家公司确实都是垄断企业。该委员会认为，在未来，司法和立法机构应该明确他们对市场主导地位的定义，以便在小公司挑战这些大型竞争对手的行动时给予它们更多的地位。
“为解决这方面的疑虑，小组委员会工作人员建议国会考虑扩大谢尔曼法，以禁止滥用支配地位。此外，小组委员会应研究设立一项法定推定，规定30%或以上的市场占有率构成可推翻的卖方支配地位推定，而25%或以上的市场占有率构成可推翻的买方支配地位推定。”
另一个有趣的部分-也可能是对寻求退出业务的投资者和初创公司创始人来说最令人担忧的部分-涉及监管机构应该如何处理未来来自大型科技公司的并购。
在这里，司法委员会建议，默认的观点应该是否决老牌科技公司…涉及初创公司的交易。哪个…。哎呀。
“由于初创企业可能是潜在和新生竞争的重要来源，反垄断法也应该对收购创新初创企业的现任者不利。国会可以这样做的一种方式是制定一项推定，禁止占主导地位的公司收购初创公司，特别是那些作为直接竞争对手的公司，以及那些在邻近或相关市场运营的公司。
在很大程度上，监管机构的话似乎是，他们应该采取更多、更早的措施来限制大型科技公司的权力，但不会采取实际上会限制大型技术公司权力的措施。
取而代之的是，他们在惩罚企业家，在已经取得市场主导地位的公司背后拉高梯子。这使得任何一家公司都更难通过自己的并购战略真正发起现实挑战。
这些规定似乎会让Snap更难达成战略交易，从而与Facebook展开更直接的竞争(这只是一个随机的例子)。
此外，一些最具战略性的收购创造了反竞争行为的机会，但这些收购并不明显。例如，Facebook对Onavo的收购可能永远不会受到审查。多亏了TechCrunch等出版物的报道，该公司技术的滥用或滥用才得以曝光。
因此，所有小时的证词、数百万份文件以及调查中的每一点劳动的结果都很简单-这是对监管机构的告诫，要求他们做得更好。
事实上，监管机构确实需要做得更好。国会应该做得更好，而这本来是很重要的。
note_en:
For startups, the most relevant points are the potential solutions the committee proposes for addressing big tech and they primarily boil down to giving small companies the benefit of the doubt when they claim that bigger rivals are exercising monopolistic advantages — and prevent the kinds of acquisitions in the future that allowed these companies to reach the unassailable positions they currently occupy in their chosen markets.
 The Committee asserts that in their core areas of business: search, ecommerce, social networking and mobile development platforms and applications, each of the companies is, indeed, a monopoly. And the committee argues that in the future judicial and legislative bodies should define down their definition of market dominance to give smaller companies more standing in cases where they challenge the actions of these large competitors.
   “To address this concern, Subcommittee staff recommends that Congress consider extending the Sherman Act to prohibit abuses of dominance.Furthermore, the Subcommittee should examine the creation of a statutory presumption that a market share of 30% or more constitutes a rebuttable presumption of dominance by a seller, and a market share of 25% or more constitute a rebuttable presumption of dominance by a buyer.”
 The other interesting section — and the one that will likely prove most troubling for investors and startup founders who are looking to exit their businesses relates to how regulators should handle future mergers and acquisitions from big technology companies.
 Here, the Judiciary Committee suggests that the default view should be to rule against transactions involving startups by established tech companies… which… yikes.
  “Since startups can be an important source of potential and nascent competition, the antitrust laws should also look unfavorably upon incumbents purchasing innovative startups. One way that Congress could do so is by codifying a presumption against acquisitions of startups by dominant firms, particularly those that serve as direct competitors, as well as those operating in adjacent or related markets.”
 For the most part, it seems that the word from regulators is that they should have done more, sooner, to limit the power of big tech, but won’t go so far as to take steps that would actually limit the power of big tech.
 Instead, they’re punishing entrepreneurs and pulling up the ladder behind the companies that have already achieved market dominance. And are making it tougher for any company to actually mount a realistic challenge through an M&A strategy of its own.
 These regulations seem like they’ll make it harder for  Snap   to make strategic deals that could put it in more direct competition with  Facebook   (just a random example).
 Furthermore, some of the most strategic acquisitions, which create the opportunities for anti-competitive behavior aren’t obvious. Facebook’s acquisition of  Onavo,   for instance, likely would never have gone up for review. It’s only thanks to reporting from publications like TechCrunch, that the misuse or abuse of the company’s technology was even revealed.
 So, the result of all of the hours of testimony, millions of documents, and every other bit of labor that went into the investigation the results are simply — an exhortation for regulators to #bebetter.
 Regulators do, indeed, need to be better. Congress should have done a better job when it would have mattered at all.

title: Daily Crunch：G Suite成为Google Workspace
title_en: Daily Crunch: G Suite becomes Google Workspace

note:
Segars指出，两家公司在2020年5月左右开始认真谈判，尽管一开始只有一小群高管参与。他说，NVIDIA真的是第一个真正为公司发挥作用的追求者-当然，软银是个例外，它在2016年将ARM私有化-他认为，在这个时间点上，将两家公司合并是非常有意义的。
他说：“他们有一个飞快的增长。他们一直在努力做到这一点，“西格斯说。“因此，考虑到他们所处的位置、我们所处的位置，思考人工智能的未来，以及它将如何无处不在，以及这如何需要更复杂的硬件-以及开发人员可以在其上开发产品的更复杂的软件环境，这是很有意义的。”这两者结合在一起，在这个时刻很有意义。“。
数据中心市场，NVIDIA也已经是这个市场的主要参与者，也是ARM近年来高度关注的一个领域。虽然它与英特尔这样的公司竞争，但西格斯对此持乐观态度。他说：“我们不是来当小角色的。”“我们的目标是获得实质性的市场份额，我认为布丁的证据就在那里。”
他还预计，在几年内，我们将看到ARM驱动的服务器在所有主要云中可用。目前，AWS凭借其定制的Gravitron处理器在这场游戏中处于领先地位。微软和谷歌目前没有提供基于ARM的服务器。
“随着时间的推移，越来越多的云所需的软件基础设施被移植到ARM上，并针对ARM进行了优化。因此，这肯定会成为一个越来越有说服力的命题，“他说，并列举了性能和能效作为云提供商使用ARM芯片的原因。
这笔交易的另一个有趣的方面是，我们可能会看到ARM也出售了NVIDIA的一些IP。对于NVIDIA来说，这将是一个巨大的变化-也是第一个，但Segars认为这样做很有意义。
“可能是NVIDIA的产品组合中有一些目前作为芯片出售的东西，我们可能会看着就走，‘你知道，如果我们把它打包成IP产品，而不修改它会怎么样？这是有市场的。或者，如果我们把它与我们正在做的其他事情结合起来，我们就可以制造出更好的产品，或者为这项技术扩大市场。我认为后者会比前者更多，因为我们所有的产品都是以知识产权的形式交付的。“。
虽然他承认NVIDIA和ARM仍面临一些监管障碍，但他相信这笔交易最终将是有利于竞争的-监管机构也会以同样的方式看待这一点。
顺便说一句，他认为该公司不会面临中国公司因为出口限制而无法许可ARM的设计的任何问题，这是交易首次宣布时许多人担心的问题。
他说：“一个产品的出口管制完全是关于它在哪里设计的，是谁设计的。”“当然，仅仅因为你的母公司改变了，并不会改变基础产品的那些基本属性。所以我们分析我们所有的产品，看看里面有多少美国成分，我们的产品在多大程度上受到美国的出口管制，英国的出口管制，以及其他出口管制制度的影响？这是一项全天候的工作，以确保我们能做到这一点。“。
TechCrunch：跟我讲讲这笔交易是怎么达成的？你的时间表是什么？
note_en:
Segars noted that the two companies started talking in earnest around May 2020, though at first, only a small group of executives was involved. Nvidia, he said, was really the first suitor to make a real play for the company — with the exception of  SoftBank,   of course, which took Arm private back in 2016 — and combining the two companies, he believes, simply makes a lot of sense at this point in time.
 “They’ve had a meteoric rise. They’ve been building up to that,” Segars said. “So it just made a lot of sense with where they are at, where we are at and thinking about the future of AI and how it’s going to go everywhere and how that necessitates much more sophisticated hardware — and a much more sophisticated software environment on which developers can build products. The combination of the two makes a lot of sense in this moment.”
 The data center market, where Nvidia, too, is already a major player, is also an area where Arm has heavily focused in recent years. And while it goes up against the likes of  Intel,   Segars is optimistic. “We’re not in it to be a bit player,” he said. “Our goal is to get a material market share and I think the proof to the pudding is there.”
 He also expects that in a few years, we’ll see Arm-powered servers available on all of the major clouds. Right now,  AWS   is ahead in this game with its  custom-built Gravitron processors. Microsoft and Google do not currently offer Arm-based servers.
  “With each passing day, more and more of the software infrastructure that’s required for the cloud is getting ported over and optimized for Arm. So it becomes a more and more compelling proposition for sure,” he said, and cited both performance and energy efficiency as reasons for cloud providers to use Arm chips.
 Another interesting aspect of the deal is that we may just see Arm sell some of Nvidia’s IP as well. That would be a big change — and a first — for Nvidia, but Segars believes it makes a lot of sense to do so.
 “It may be that there is something in the portfolio of Nvidia that they currently sell as a chip that we may look at and go, ‘you know, what if we package that up as an IP product, without modifying it? There’s a market for that.’ Or it may be that there’s a thing in here where if we take that and combine it with something else that we were doing, we can make a better product or expand the market for the technology. I think it’s going to be more of the latter than it is the former because we design all our products to be delivered as IP.”
 And while he acknowledged that Nvidia and Arm still face some regulatory hurdles, he believes the deal will be pro-competitive in the end — and that the regulators will see it the same way.
 He does not believe, by the way, that the company will face any issues with Chinese companies not being able to license Arm’s designs because of export restrictions, something a lot of people were worried about when the deal was first announced.
 “Export control of a product is all about where was it designed and who designed it,” he said. “And of course, just because your parent company changes, doesn’t change those fundamental properties of the underlying product. So we analyze all our products and look at how much U.S. content is in there, to what extent are our products subject to U.S. export control, U.K. export control, other export control regimes? It’s a full-time piece of work to make sure we stay on top of that.”
  TechCrunch: Walk me through how that deal came about? What was the timeline for you?
title: ARM首席执行官西蒙·西格斯讨论人工智能、数据中心、被NVIDIA收购等问题
title_en: Arm CEO Simon Segars discusses AI, data centers, getting acquired by Nvidia and more

note:
随着大流行改变了我们对最后一英里物流的看法，真正零接触送货的前景似乎比以往任何时候都更近，也更重要。来自联邦快递、Postmate和折射AI的自动递送高管加入了我们的行列，在TechCrunch Mobility 2020上谈论了这个新兴领域。
联邦快递高级技术和创新副总裁丽贝卡·杨(Rebecca Yeung)解释了为什么这家物流巨头认为是时候在自治领域加倍试验了。
她说：“COVID带来了‘非接触式’这一术语--在此之前，没有多少人谈论非接触式；现在，它几乎是我们首选的交付方式。”“因此，我们看到，从政府到消费者，都持开放的态度，也许在未来，你会让所有的东西都通过自主的方式交付给你，这是首选的方式。”
“如果你在推特或Instagram上查找PostMates机器人，人们总是在质疑，这是什么？它在做什么？有了COVID，一切都在一夜之间改变了，人们看到机器人就会立即明白，哦，这是用于非接触式送货的，“PostMates特别项目副总裁Ali Kashani说。“一切都突然变得有意义了。”
他还解释了特定于送餐的机器人平台的表面限制是如何使工程过程(如果不是更容易的话)至少自然地受到他们收集的数据的约束的。
卡沙尼说：“这在某种程度上是如此贴近市场的优势之一，我们可以使用我们平台上的数据来推动某些决策，因为你不想过度设计，也不想低于设计。”“我们实际上开发了模拟，可以在过去的某个日期把机器人放在这个国家的任何地方。它会告诉我们，这个机器人送了多少次货？外面有几个小时？它行驶了多少英里？它将使用这些信息来准确地决定我们需要什么样的电池寿命？需要带饮料吗？应该有多少酒水持有者才能覆盖99%的送货量呢？
折射AI的联合创始人兼首席技术官马修·约翰逊-罗伯逊(Matthew Johnson-Roberson)指出，大流行提高了人们的兴趣和需求，但也强调了事情需要以不同的方式向前发展的地方。
他说：“显然，没有人想要全球大流行，但它肯定给这个行业注入了活力，并给予了更多的关注。”“每个人都很兴奋，哦，我们将实现非接触式送货，这会很棒的。但我认为，作为一个行业，要实现这一目标，还有一些真正的挑战需要解决。一种是社会接受，另一种是管制。因为COVID，这种情况开始改变了。我希望这是一个转折点，我们确实看到了更多严肃的投资，但也看到了广泛的部署，所以这不是一个你只在一个地方就能看到的技术演示，但它实际上开始接管一些相当大的市场份额。“
杨家诚还强调了对支持这些自主平台的基础设施的需求：“考虑到未来，商业投放，你需要动态路由，你需要调度系统，你需要用户界面，你需要一个跟踪界面。我们看到了巨大的协同效应，我们可以在各种自主应用中加以利用。“。
在讨论用机器人取代人类工人的危险时，杨和卡沙尼持乐观态度，他们和机器人行业的其他人一样，暗示劳动力会发生转移，但不会杀死任何工作岗位。约翰逊-罗伯逊不同意这一观点。
“我认为我们将取代工作岗位，我们需要正面面对这一点，”他说。“我认为重要的是我们要正视这一点，很多这样的决定都有很长的历史，他们没有考虑到人类将会带来什么后果。所以我是一个倡导者，说，看，我们正在取代工作。让我们作为一个社会来思考：我们如何解决这个问题？我们该怎么处理呢？我认为，我们可以生活在一个拥有更公正、更公平的工作、拥有医疗保险和更多福利的未来。但我认为它不会是今天看起来的样子。“
note_en:
The prospect of truly zero contact delivery seems closer — and more important — than ever with the pandemic changing how we think of last mile logistics. Autonomous delivery executives from  FedEx,   Postmates, and Refraction AI joined us to talk about the emerging field at TechCrunch Mobility 2020.
 FedEx VP of Advanced Technology and Innovation Rebecca Yeung explained why the logistics giant felt that it was time to double down on its experiments in the area of autonomy.
  “COVID brought the term ‘contactless’ — before that not many people are talking about contactless; Now it’s almost a preferred way of us delivering,” she said. “So we see, from government to consumers, open mindedness about, maybe in the future you would have everything delivered to you through autonomous means, and that’s the preferred way.”
 “If you looked up  Postmates   robots on Twitter or Instagram, people are always kind of questioning, what is this? What is it doing? Everything changed overnight with COVID, where people would see the robot and immediately understand, oh, this is for contactless delivery,” said Postmates VP of special projects Ali Kashani. “Everything suddenly made sense.”
  He also explained how the seeming constraints of a robotic platform specific to food delivery made the engineering process, if not easier, at least naturally bounded by the data they’d collected.
 “It’s kind of one of the advantages of being so close to the market, we can use data from our platform to drive certain decisions, because you don’t want to over-engineer you also don’t want to under-engineer,” Kashani said. “We actually developed simulations that would put robots in any location in the country on some date in the past. It would tell us, how many deliveries did this robot do? How many hours was it outside? How many miles did it travel? And it would use that information to decide exactly what kind of battery life do we need? Does it need to carry drinks? How many drink holders should it have to cover 99% of deliveries?”
 Matthew Johnson-Roberson, co-founder and CTO of  Refraction AI,   noted that the pandemic has raised interest and demand, but also highlighted where things need to move forward in different ways.
  “Obviously no one wants a global pandemic, but it has certainly energized this industry and put more attention on it,” he said. “Everybody is excited, oh, we’re going to have contactless delivery, it’s going to be great. But I think there are some real challenges that need to be addressed as an industry to get there. One of them is social acceptance, the other’s regulation. That’s starting to change because of COVID. I’m hopeful that this is an inflection point, and that we really do see more serious investment in this, but also widespread deployment, so it’s not a tech demo that you get to see once in one place, but it actually begins to take over some sizable bit of the market.”
 Yeung also emphasized the need for the infrastructure that supports these autonomous platforms: “Thinking about the future, commercial launch, you need the dynamic routing, you need the dispatch system, you need the user interface, you need a tracking interface. We see great synergy for us to leverage for all sorts of autonomous applications.”
  In discussing the danger of replacing human workers with robots, Yeung and Kashani were sanguine, suggesting like others in the robotics industry that there would be a shift in labor but it won’t kill any jobs. Johnson-Roberson disagreed.
 “I think we are going to be replacing jobs, and we need to face that head on,” he said. “I think it’s important that we reckon with that, that a lot of these decisions, they have a long history of not thinking through what hte human consequences will be. So I’m an advocate for saying, look, we’re replacing jobs. Let’s think as a society: How do we address that? How do we deal with it? I think that we could live in a future with more just, fairer jobs with health insurance, more benefits. But I don’t think it is going to look how it looks today.”

在周二晚间的一份声明中，印度电子和信息技术部(MeitY)表示，这些公司将在未来五年生产价值超过1430亿美元的智能手机和其他电子零部件。作为回报，印度政府将在五年内为他们提供4%至6%的激励措施，鼓励他们在五年内额外销售当地生产的商品，并将2019年至2020年定为基准年。
新德里此举旨在大幅提高印度的制造和出口能力，并在当地创造更多就业机会。印度商务部说，当地生产的产品中约有60%将出口。商务部表示，未来五年，这些公司将创造20多万个直接就业机会，同期将创造多达60万个间接就业机会。
此举也预示着，未来几年，印度这个全球第二大市场的主要智能手机制造商之间的动态可能会发生怎样的变化。富士康、纬创和和硕的加入突显出苹果计划如何迅速扩大其在印度的本地制造能力。纬创三年前开始在印度组装少量iPhone机型，富士康紧随其后。和硕尚未在印度开始生产。
“苹果和三星合计占全球手机销售收入的近60%，这一计划预计将增加他们在中国的制造基地，”商务部表示。
工业界对印度作为世界级制造业目的地取得的辉煌进步抱有信心，这与印度总理阿特玛·尼尔巴尔·巴拉特(AtmaNirbhar Bharat)的号角-一个自力更生的印度-产生了强烈的共鸣。
印度公司Lava，Bhagwati(Micromax)，Padget Electronics，UTL Neolyncs和Optiemus Electronics也获得了批准。但名单上没有中国智能手机制造商Oppo，Vivo，OnePlus和Realme，它们没有申请该项目。中国智能手机厂商目前占据了印度市场约80%的份额。三星曾是印度智能手机市场的领头羊，近年来面临着来自小米和Vivo的激烈竞争。
note_en:
In a  statement Tuesday evening, Indian Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) said these companies will be producing smartphones and other electronics components worth more than $143 billion over the next five years. In return, India will offer them an incentive of 4% to 6% on additional sales of goods produced locally over five years, with 2019-2020 set as the base year.
 New Delhi’s move is aimed at significantly improving India’s manufacturing and exporting capacities and generating more local jobs. Around 60% of the locally produced products will be exported, the Indian ministry said. The companies will generate more than 200,000 direct employment opportunities in next five years and as many as 600,000 indirect employment opportunities during the same period, the ministry said.
  The move is also a precursor to how the dynamics among major smartphone makers might change in India, the world’s second largest market, over the next few years. The inclusion of  Foxconn,   Wistron and  Pegatron   underscores how rapidly Apple plans to expand its local manufacturing capabilities in India. Wistron began assembling a handful of iPhone models in India three years ago,  followed by Foxconn. Pegatron has yet to start production in India.
 “Apple and Samsung together account for nearly 60% of global sales revenue of mobile phones and this scheme is expected to increase their manufacturing base manifold in the country,” the ministry said.
 “Industry has reposed its faith in India’s stellar progress as a world class manufacturing destination and this resonates strongly with Prime Minister’s clarion call of AtmaNirbhar Bharat – a self-reliant India,” the ministry added.
 Indian firms Lava, Bhagwati (Micromax), Padget Electronics, UTL Neolyncs and Optiemus Electronics have also received the approval. But missing from the list are Chinese smartphone makers Oppo, Vivo, OnePlus and Realme that had not applied for the program. Chinese smartphone vendors currently  command about 80% of the Indian market. Samsung, which once  led the Indian smartphone market, has faced intense competition from Xiaomi and Vivo in recent years.

该项目的规模表明了赫尔辛基创业生态系统的信心和雄心，该生态系统在过去10-15年里取得了不可估量的增长。
事实上，涉及到Slush，一个会议，并不是偶然的。这项活动是由大学创建的非营利性活动。这使得它能够扩大到欧洲最大的科技活动之一(Pre-COVID)，有超过2万人参加。它的成功也让传统上保守的芬兰人拥抱创业精神。整个城市都参与其中，数以千计的大学生志愿为城市和生态系统服务。冰雪体验的大学性质反映了赫尔辛基是如何双手抓住科技机遇的。
Slaush诞生于阿尔托大学(Alalto University)及其创业学生协会，最初是一个技术聚会。事实上，我参加了一些首批活动。但随着2010年当地初创公司Rovio(“愤怒的小鸟”的创造者)和Supercell(“部落冲突”的创造者)的成功，这场活动开始了。彼得·维斯特巴卡(之前是惠普Bazaar实验室的先驱)是雪糕的不知疲倦的推动者，这一点起到了帮助作用，并推动它从一个会议变成了一个全面的会议，可能会吸引科技界的大牌人物。
斯莱什在隆冬将风投吸引到北欧国家的能力令人印象深刻。这座城市铺上了红地毯。这意味着流入的风投出现了爆炸式增长。根据芬兰风险投资协会(FVCA)的数据，2014年至2018年间，芬兰的风险投资增长了近五倍，达到1.88亿欧元。就风险资本占GDP的百分比而言，芬兰现在是欧洲的领先者(FVCA说)，外国风险投资在2017-18年间增长了58%。
风险投资在这座城市本身也取得了突飞猛进的增长。CrunchBase列出了赫尔辛基54家各种形式的风险基金。它们包括Conor Venture Partners、Inenture、VNT Management、IceBreaker.vc、SuperHero Capital、Evli Growth Partners、OpenOcean、LoudSpring、Norsepower Oy、TESI、NordicNinja VC和Maki.vc。
斯利什甚至看到前雇员创办了大型初创公司。由Slush早期首席执行官Miki Kuusi联合创立的外卖初创公司Wolt已经筹集了1.6亿美元。赫尔辛基生态系统中的其他大型初创公司包括Smartly、Singa、Giosg、ZenRobotics和Blok。我们不要忘记，它在当年就产生了MySQL和CRF Health。2018年，Small Giant Games，被Zynga以高达7亿美元的交易收购。
Startup Genome将赫尔辛基标记为全球顶级生态系统之一。2020年，它对赫尔辛基初创生态系统的估值为58亿美元，早期资金总额为5.11亿美元，高于新兴生态系统的全球平均水平。
赫尔辛基大约有250家游戏企业，其中30家的年销售额超过100万美元。2017年，芬兰是第一个发布国家人工智能战略的欧盟国家，赫尔辛基大学创建了一个免费的人工智能教育项目，前四个月有来自80个不同国家的约9万人注册。
在整个国家，近30万芬兰人从事科技工作，考虑到赫尔辛基的130万人口，这是一个巨大的数字。
一般来说，你对投资哪些趋势最感兴趣？我们是一个多面手，但热衷于B2C和B2B领域的深度技术和品牌驱动型公司。我们一直在密切关注新的基于材料的创新，以及量子计算方面的突破性创新。其他地方也出现了突破，[我们]已经投资了B2B SaaS和一家云本地的大型多人游戏公司。
你最近最令人兴奋的投资是什么？最近的一项投资是对一家名为Carbon Cloud的瑞典公司的投资。它们可以很容易地发现你的气候足迹并向世界展示-例如，它们可以在Oatly的包装一侧找到。消费品的二氧化碳影响应该和食品中的营养价值一样明显。
有没有一些你希望在这个行业里看到但没有的初创公司？现在有哪些被忽视的机会？Femtech公司。竞争仍然很少，但有大量的工作要做。我们的团队热衷于看到生殖健康方面的更多解决方案，但也超越了解决方案，例如，将女性的个人周期与最佳营养或培训同步。
一般说来，你在下一笔投资中希望得到什么？团队始终处于中心位置，我们正在寻找在全球市场改写未来的企业家。
在这一点上，对于一家新的创业公司来说，哪些领域要么是过饱和的，要么是太难竞争的？您还担心或担心哪些其他类型的产品/服务？免费游戏是一个竞争激烈的市场。可能会有新的赢家，但与许多其他行业相比，也会有更多的公司未能成功。
与其他创业中心(或其他地方)相比，你在当地生态系统上的投资集中了多少？超过50%？较少?。我们有全球授权，但北欧是我们的家，也是我们进行大部分投资的地方。
你所在的城市和地区的哪些行业似乎处于有利地位，可以长期蓬勃发展，或者不会？你对哪些公司感到兴奋(你的投资组合是否如此)，哪些创始人？有几个，但首先想到的是可持续性和新材料。斯普诺瓦是一个很好的例子，为纺织业提供了世界上最可持续的纤维，以合理的成本生产出对环境危害最小的纤维。凭借棉花的伸长和强力，以及羊绒的绝缘性，它可以适用于服装、鞋类、配饰和家用纺织品等等。我也期待着看到一个伟大的生态系统和几个围绕量子计算建立的初创公司。已经有一些前景看好的量子技术公司，例如制造世界级量子计算机的芬兰IQM公司和专门研究用于量子计算的无制冷剂稀释制冷机系统的Bluefors公司。
其他城市的投资者应该如何看待你所在城市的整体投资环境和机会？芬兰有强大的创业支持生态系统，强大的工程历史和伟大的做事文化。
你预计未来几年会有更多的创始人来自大城市以外的地区吗？由于流行病和挥之不去的担忧，加上远程工作的吸引力，初创中心会流失人员吗？创业生态系统的美妙之处在于它建立在创新的基础上。我们很可能会在未来看到更多的分布式组织，但我相信主要的枢纽在未来也会保持它们的吸引力。
由于新冠肺炎的原因，您投资的哪些行业领域看起来更弱，或者更容易受到消费者和企业行为潜在变化的影响？在这个史无前例的时代，初创企业可能会利用哪些机会？旅行和招待自然受到了很大的打击。该行业需要时间才能完全复苏，我预计未来该领域将出现创新，无论是在虚拟旅行领域，还是在未来建立对无忧旅行的信心。
新冠肺炎对您的投资策略有何影响？在你的投资组合中，创始人最大的担忧是什么？你现在对你投资组合中的初创公司有什么建议？对许多人来说，延长跑道一直是一条普遍规则，当情况重新开始好转时，它会使公司变得更强大、更具竞争力。
你是否在你的投资组合中看到了收入增长、留住或其他适应大流行的势头的“萌芽”？客户支持人员在大流行期间一直处于紧张状态。我们的投资组合公司Ultimate.ai已处于有利地位，可以利用其虚拟代理扩展客户支持，同时保持甚至改善客户体验。我非常高兴地看到它们迅速成长和扩张。
在过去一个月左右的时间里，是什么时刻给了你希望？这可以是专业的，也可以是个人的，或者两者兼而有之。
note_en:
The scale of the project speaks to the confidence and ambition of the Helsinki startup ecosystem, which has grown immeasurably over the last 10-15 years.
 The fact that  Slush,   a conference, is involved is no accident. The event is a nonprofit created by the university. This has enabled it to scale to one of Europe’s largest tech events (pre-COVID) at over 20,000 attendees. Its success has also led to traditionally conservative Finns embracing entrepreneurship. The entire city gets involved, and thousands of university students volunteer for the good of the city and the ecosystem. The collegiate nature of the Slush experience has reflected how Helsinki has grabbed the opportunities of tech with both hands.
 Born of Aalto University and its student society for entrepreneurship, AaltoES, Slush originally started as a tech meetup. Indeed, I went to some of the first ones. But with the 2010’s success of local startup Rovio, creator of Angry Birds, as well as Supercell, creator of Clash of Clans, the event took off. It helped that  Peter Vesterbacka (previously a pioneer at HP Bazaar Labs) was a tireless promoter of Slush and egged it on from being a meetup into a full-blown conference that could attract the biggest names in tech.
 Slush’s ability to attract VCs to a Northern European country in the middle of winter was impressive. The city rolled out the red carpet. That meant inbound VC exploded. According to the Finnish Venture Capital Association (FVCA), VC investment into Finland grew almost five times to €188 million between 2014 and 2018. Finland is now a European leader in terms of venture capital (says the FVCA) as a percentage of GDP, and foreign VC investments grew by 58% between 2017-18.
 VC has grown leaps and bounds in the city itself. Crunchbase lists  54 venture funds of various guises in Helsinki. They include Conor Venture Partners, Inventure, VNT Management, Icebreaker.vc, Superhero Capital, Evli Growth Partners, OpenOcean, Loudspring, Norsepower Oy, Tesi, NordicNinja VC and Maki.vc.
 Slush has even seen ex-employees go on to found big startups. Food delivery startup Wolt, co-founded by Miki Kuusi an early CEO of Slush, has raised $160 million. Other big startup companies from Helsinki’s ecosystem include Smartly, Singa, Giosg, ZenRobotics and Blok. And let’s not forget it produced MySQL and CRF Health back in the day. In 2018, Small Giant Games, was acquired by Zynga in a deal worth up to $700 million.
 Startup Genome marks out Helsinki as one of the top global ecosystems. For 2020, it valued the Helsinki startup ecosystem at $5.8 billion, with total early-stage funding of $511 million, higher than the global average for emerging ecosystems.
 Helsinki has around 250 gaming enterprises and 30 of them exceed $1 million in annual sales. In 2017 Finland was the first EU country to publish a national AI strategy and the University of Helsinki created a free AI education program that saw approximately 90,000 people from 80 different countries enroll in the first four months.
 Over the whole country, nearly 300,000 Finns work in tech, an enormous amount when you consider the population of Helsinki is 1.3 million.
      What trends are you most excited about investing in, generally? We are a generalist but are keen on deep tech and brand-driven companies both in B2C and B2B. We have been tracking closely new materials-based innovations, as well as breakthrough innovations in quantum computing. Breakthroughs happen also elsewhere and [we] have invested in B2B SaaS as well as one cloud-native massive multiplayer game company.
 What’s your latest, most exciting investment? One the latest investments is in a Swedish company called Carbon Cloud. They make it easy to discover your climate footprint and show it to the world — they can be found, for example, on the side of Oatly’s packaging. Carbon dioxide impact of consumer goods should be as visible as the nutrition values in food.
 Are there startups that you wish you would see in the industry but don’t? What are some overlooked opportunities right now? Femtech. There’s still quite little competition, but tremendous amount of work to do. Our team is keen to see more solutions on reproductive health, but also going beyond to solutions e.g., in syncing female’s personal cycle with optimal nutrition or training.
 What are you looking for in your next investment, in general? The team is always in the center and we are looking for entrepreneurs that are rewriting the future in global markets.
 Which areas are either oversaturated or would be too hard to compete in at this point for a new startup? What other types of products/services are you wary or concerned about? Free-to-play games is a tough and competitive market. There will likely be new winners, but also even greater number of companies that don’t make it compared to many other industries.
 How much are you focused on investing in your local ecosystem versus other startup hubs (or everywhere) in general? More than 50%? Less? We have a global mandate, but the Nordics is our home and where we have done most of our investments.
 Which industries in your city and region seem well-positioned to thrive, or not, long term? What are companies you are excited about (your portfolio or not), which founders? There are several, but the first on the top of mind is sustainability and new materials. Spinnova is a great example providing the textile industry with the most sustainable fibre in the world, produced with minimal harm to the environment, at a reasonable cost. With the stretch and strength qualities of cotton and the insulation of lamb’s wool, it can suit apparel, footwear, accessories, [and] home textiles to name a few applications. I’m also looking forward to seeing a great ecosystem and several startups being built around quantum computing. There are already a number of promising quantum technology companies, such as the Finnish IQM that builds world-class quantum computers and Bluefors that specialize in cryogen-free dilution refrigerator systems for quantum computing.
 How should investors in other cities think about the overall investment climate and opportunities in your city? There is strong supporting ecosystem in Finland for startups, strong engineering history and great culture of getting things done.
 Do you expect to see a surge in more founders coming from geographies outside major cities in the years to come, with startup hubs losing people due to the pandemic and lingering concerns, plus the attraction of remote work? The beauty of the startup ecosystem is that is built on innovation. We will most likely see more distributed organizations in the future, but I believe the major hubs will maintain their attractiveness in the future as well.
 Which industry segments that you invest in look weaker or more exposed to potential shifts in consumer and business behavior because of COVID-19? What are the opportunities startups may be able to tap into during these unprecedented times? Travel and hospitality has naturally taken a big hit. It will take time for the industry to fully recover and I would expect innovations in the domain in the future, whether it is in virtual travel or creating confidence in worry-free travel in the future.
 How has COVID-19 impacted your investment strategy? What are the biggest worries of the founders in your portfolio? What is your advice to startups in your portfolio right now? Extending the runway has been a general rule for many and making the company stronger and more competitive when things start picking up again.
 Are you seeing “green shoots” regarding revenue growth, retention or other momentum in your portfolio as they adapt to the pandemic? Customer support agents have been strained during the pandemic. Our portfolio company Ultimate.ai has been well-positioned to scale the customer support with their virtual agents while maintaining or even improving customer experience. I’ve been super happy to see them grow and expand rapidly.
 What is a moment that has given you hope in the last month or so? This can be professional, personal or a mix of the two.

今天，一名男孩和他的母亲对任天堂提起集体诉讼，原因是任天堂在解决任天堂交换机控制器中常见的硬件问题方面做得不够。这是与“Joy-Con漂移”问题相关的几项法律努力之一，“Joy-Con漂移”是一种现象，即Switch Joy-Con控制器即使在没有人移动的情况下也会让游戏中的角色“漂移”。
起诉书在北加州提起，起诉者是一位名叫卢兹·桑切斯(Luz Sanchez)的妇女和她9岁或10岁的儿子，他在法庭文件中被称为M.S.。起诉书描述了桑切斯是如何在2018年12月给儿子买了一台任天堂(Nintendo)交换机的，当时他8岁。起诉书称，在一个月内，桑切斯的操控者开始登记游戏中的动作，而他的手不在手边。不到一年后，它说，“Joy-Con漂移变得如此明显，以至于控制器变得不能用于一般的游戏用途。”桑切斯的母亲慷慨地购买了另一套控制器，但起诉书称，七个月后，它们也开始漂移。
Joy-Con漂移在开关设备中普遍存在。(据传闻，我曾在我自己的两套控制器上体验过这一点)。字符向左或向右英寸，就像幽灵在操作控制台一样。任天堂直到2019年7月才承认这个问题。当月，任天堂Switch Subreddit上一条呼吁Joy-Con漂移的帖子获得了超过2.5万张赞成票。当时，十几名交换机所有者可能提起集体诉讼，称Joy-Cons是“有缺陷的”。律师们表示，任天堂听到用户抱怨的时间已经够长了；该公司为什么不披露这一问题？
2019年的诉讼已经进入仲裁程序，原告律师最近要求Switch用户提交视频，描述他们使用Joy-Con漂移的经历，以帮助支持他们的案件。上个月，一个法国消费者团体也提出了申诉，声称计划中的过时。
任天堂从2019年7月开始在保修后免费修复Joy-Cons，任天堂总裁在今年夏天的一次财务会议上为这个问题道歉。但桑切斯的律师辩称，任天堂在解决问题或预先警告客户方面做得不够。起诉书中写道：“被告在完全了解产品缺陷的情况下继续营销和销售产品，而没有在营销、促销或包装过程中向消费者披露Joy-Con Drift缺陷。”被告隐瞒缺陷是有经济动机的，因为它不想停止销售产品，和/或需要花费一大笔钱来修复缺陷。
原告要求五百多万美元的损害赔偿金。截至发稿时，任天堂和桑切斯的律师均拒绝置评。
目前还不清楚这起案件是否也会进行仲裁，但原告提出了一个关于任天堂责任的重要问题。布法罗大学法学院(University Of Buffalo School Of Law)法学教授克里斯汀·巴塞洛缪(Christine Bartholomew)表示：“企业有义务披露可能改变产品价值的产品信息。”“如果你要买某个价格的东西，如果你知道它会在六个月后破损，那么产品的价值就会大不相同。如果一家公司掌握了这些信息，但没有分享，这将被视为法律范围内的不当行为。“。
MS甚至不是第一个起诉任天堂的9岁小孩。1990年，一个孩子起诉美国职业棒球大联盟和任天堂，因为他得到的40美元的棒球比赛没有达到他的期望。Joy-Con漂移的主观性较小，另外，更换它们需要花费80美元。

note_en:
A boy and his mother today filed a class action lawsuit against Nintendo for not doing enough to fix a hardware problem common among Nintendo Switch controllers. It is one of several legal efforts related to the issue of “Joy-Con drift”—a phenomenon where the Switch Joy-Con controllers make in-game characters “drift” even when nobody is moving them.
 The complaint, filed in Northern California, was brought by a woman named Luz Sanchez and her 9- or 10-year-old son, who, as a minor, is referred to in court documents as M.S. The complaint describes how Sanchez purchased her son a Nintendo Switch in December 2018, when he was 8. Within a month, the complaint alleges, Sanchez’s controllers began registering in-game movement when his hands weren’t on them. Less than a year later, it says, “the Joy-Con drift became so pronounced that the controllers became inoperable for general gameplay use.” Sanchez’s mom obligingly purchased another set of controllers, but seven months later, the complaint alleges, they began drifting too.
 Joy-Con drift is pervasive among Switch devices. (Anecdotally, I’ve experienced it on two sets of my own controllers). Characters inch left or right as if a ghost was operating the console. Nintendo didn’t acknowledge the problem much until July 2019. That month, a thread on the Nintendo Switch subreddit calling out Joy-Con drift received over 25,000 upvotes. More than a dozen Switch owners filed a potential class action lawsuit at the time  calling Joy-Cons “defective.” Lawyers said Nintendo had heard users’ complaints for long enough; why didn’t the company disclose the issue?
  The 2019 lawsuit has been moved into arbitration, and the plaintiffs' lawyers recently  asked Switch users to submit videos describing their experiences with Joy-Con drift to help bolster their case. Last month, a French consumer group  filed a complaint, too, alleging planned obsolescence.
  Nintendo  began fixing Joy-Cons for free, post-warranty, in July 2019, and Nintendo’s president  apologized for the problem in a financial meeting this summer. But Sanchez’s lawyers argue that Nintendo hasn’t done enough to fix the issue or warn customers about it up front. “Defendant continues to market and sell the Products with full knowledge of the defect and without disclosing the Joy-Con Drift defect to consumers in its marketing, promotion, or packaging,” the complaint reads. “Defendant has had a financial motive to conceal the defect, as it did not want to stop selling the Products, and/or would need to expend a significant amount of money to cure the defect.”
  The plaintiffs are asking for over $5,000,000 in damages. Nintendo and Sanchez’s lawyers declined to comment by press time.
 It’s unclear whether this case will head for arbitration as well, but the plaintiffs have raised an important question about Nintendo’s responsibilities. “Businesses are obligated to disclose information about a product that would change the value of the product,” says Christine Bartholomew, a law professor at the University of Buffalo School of Law. “If you’re going to buy something that’s a certain price, the value of the product would be quite different if you knew it would break in six months. If a company has that information and doesn’t share it, that would be considered misconduct within the reach of law.”
 M.S. isn’t even the first 9-year-old to sue Nintendo. In 1990 a kid  sued both Major League Baseball and Nintendo because the $40 baseball game he got didn’t live up to his expectations. Joy-Con drift is less subjective—plus, they cost $80 to replace.

传奇吉他手、先驱金属乐队Van Halen的领导人埃迪·范·海伦(Eddie Van Halen)在与癌症抗争后于2020年10月6日去世，享年65岁。被广泛认为是他那一代人中最伟大的吉他手-也许是有史以来最伟大的吉他手-可以毫不夸张地说，摇滚之神影响了他之后的每一位现代演奏家。范·海伦极具创造性的创新，包括敲击，或者用左手和右手同时放在脖子上弹吉他的行为，重新定义了音乐家可以用乐器做什么-以及摇滚乐可以听起来是什么样子。范海伦甚至为他的一些改变游戏规则的技术申请了专利。
范海伦在2015年为大众机械写了这篇文章，讨论了他的专利，重建了他的吉他和放大器，并寻找他标志性的声音。为了纪念他，Pop Mech正在全文转载这篇文章。愿他安息。
我一直都是个修修补补的人。这是我爸爸给我的。从小到大，我们住在帕萨迪纳市一栋没有车道的房子里。你走了一条穿过街区中央的小巷，在所有的房子后面，去你的后院或车库。我们后面的邻居有一辆U型拖车停在千斤顶上，满载着煤渣块。
一天晚上，我爸爸凌晨三点从一场演唱会回到家。他有点发烧，他喝了几杯，所以他说，这东西挡住了我再上车的路。于是他下车想把它移开。他一抬起拖车，千斤顶就倒了，把他的手指砍掉了。
这是个问题。除了显而易见的原因外，他还演奏单簧管和萨克斯管。在萨克斯上，你不需要用手指封孔。阀门就会在上面关闭。但是用单簧管，你必须密封孔，所以他拿了一个萨克斯管阀盖，改装成适合他的单簧管。
另一件有趣的事发生在他晚年，当时他开始掉牙。你需要用你的底牙来演奏簧片乐器。他没有去看牙医，而是用白色聚四氟乙烯为自己做了一个形状完美的假体，填补了他缺失的牙齿的缺口。他必须打球的时候才塞进去的。看着他做那种事让我产生了好奇心。如果某件事没有按照你的意愿去做，总有一种方法可以修复它。

我的演奏风格真的是从我承受不起变形踏板的事实中成长起来的。我不得不试着把那些声音从我的吉他里挤出来。我做的第一件真正的工作是在我的卧室里。我加了拾音器，因为我不喜欢原版的声音。
我买不起路由器--我甚至不知道路由器是什么--所以我开始用螺丝刀不停地敲打。那根本不管用。大块的木头飞走了，到处都是木屑。但我当时在执行任务。我知道我想要什么，我一直坚持到我最终到达那里。
大部分吉他琴颈的背面都太圆了，所以我拿出砂纸，把琴颈改得很平。实际上，我早些时候重新弹了几把吉他，因为我想把指板刮下来，让琴颈更平坦。它越平坦，我就能把弦弯曲得越远，而不会感到不安，也不会在弦碰到脖子上更高的颤音时把声音呛得喘不过气来。
另一个问题，至少对于挡泥板来说，是他们在脖子上涂上的透明漆。当你出汗时，你的手指要么滑得到处都是，要么变得粘乎乎的。我无法忍受这一点，所以当我制作我的第一把吉他时，我用的是天然木材。我自己的汗水和油会浸泡进去，让它变得光滑。它花了很长时间才能做到这一点，但最终，它感觉比任何你可以放在上面的合成产品都要好得多。
此内容是从YouTube导入的。您可以在他们的网站上找到不同格式的相同内容，也可以找到更多信息。
颤音棒(也叫重击棒或颤音)就是不合拍。问题出在螺母--吉他琴颈末端的弦引导。在第一张专辑中，我使用了标准的非锁定挡泥板颤音。弦从螺母到调音钉成角度向下倾斜，产生张力，当你使用重击杆时，弦来回滑动后，防止弦回到原来的槽中。我自己做的坚果有非常光滑的凹痕-又大又圆，就像船底一样。我还在里面加了一滴3合1的油，所以管子会特别滑。
最重要的是，不是把弦绕在调音钉上，形成一个角度，造成张力，我会把它缠绕起来，这样，从螺母一直到桥上，弦都是水平的。否则，当你在重击酒吧里发疯时，坚果里可能会有会让吉他跑调的插头。
唯一的问题是当你碰到一根打开的绳子时，你的手指没有按住它。如果没有这种张力，绳子就会从螺母槽中弹出来，所以我必须记住把手指放在螺母的另一边才能把东西固定在一起。
如果它是可移动的，或可旋转的，或任何类似于可以上下移动的东西，我会弄乱它，让放大器运行得更热。我打开放大器，看到了这个东西。后来我发现这是一个偏置控制，它控制输出管的功率。我四处张望，突然触摸到这个巨大的蓝色东西，我的天哪，就像被迈克·泰森一拳打在胸口一样。我的整个身体都僵硬地弯曲着，它肯定把我抛出了5英尺。我碰到了一个电容器。我不知道它们是耐电压的。
我从我工作的商店带回家的马歇尔放大器只有在你把音量调大的情况下才是好的。再低一点，你就会失去变形。我需要它，但是这么大的音量是不可能在任何地方演奏的，所以我试了所有的方法，从把厚厚的塑料盖子放在上面，把它背对着，再到把它面朝下。我每小时会烧两次保险丝。
幸运的是，不久之后，我偶然发现了Variac变压器。我又买了一台马歇尔放大器，我不知道它实际上是欧洲型号的。我插上电源，我在等它预热，然后想，我在这里被敲诈了，没有声音出来！我被激怒了，一个小时后又回来再试一次。
我的功放一直开着。我不知道它调到了220，所以当我打开吉他的时候，它听起来就像一个全音量的马歇尔，一直调到最高，只是非常非常安静。就在那时，我意识到电压有些不对劲。房子里有一些粗制滥造的调光器，我把它连接到其中一个上。
当然，我把它反向布线，整个房子都短路了，所以我去了帕萨迪纳的一个地方，问是否有某种工业尺寸的可变变压器可以让我调节电压，他们给我介绍了Variac。它只是一个巨大的调光器。我把它插到放大器里，然后从那里控制电压。那就成了我的音量旋钮。我会根据我们玩的房间的大小来设置电压，以任何音量获得所有的反馈。
我的第一把真正的吉他是Les Paul Goldtop。我是个彻头彻尾的埃里克·克莱普顿怪胎，我看过他扮演莱斯·保罗的旧照片。除了他的有隆起的皮卡，而我的有肥皂杆，P-90单线圈。我用那把吉他做的第一件事就是把它从后面凿出来，然后放进一个大圆盘。当我们在演唱会上演奏时，人们一直在说，他是怎么从单圈肥皂棒拾音器里发出那种声音的？因为我的手遮住了大喇叭，他们从来没有意识到我把它放进去了。
当我的吉他是黑白的时候，我剪下了我自己的拾音器，这样它就可以盖住我取下的皮卡上的洞。但是当我在黑白的上面刷红色的时候，就是现在的样子，它和那个黑色的扒手在一起看起来并不酷。它覆盖了大部分的粉刷工作。我决定把开关塞在中间，把一辆坏了的皮卡放在前面，因为我没有用过它。我并没有试图欺骗任何人。底线是，我不知道怎么把它钩起来。
对我来说，最后一个真正的步骤是在我的皮卡上加石蜡。拾音器可能会有这种非常高端的尖叫声，就像有时有人对着麦克风说话时听到的恼人的反馈尖叫声一样。我想，也许是吉他的线圈绕组振动造成了这种情况。所以我所做的-我不知道这个想法是从哪里来的-是买了一个热盘和石蜡砖块，然后从我妈妈那里借了一个玉班咖啡罐把蜡放进去。
当然，我弄坏了很多皮卡，因为我还没来得及把皮卡拉出来，塑料车架就会融化。但最后，当我有机会真正盯着它的时候，我一看到皮卡开始升温并有点干瘪，我就会把它拔出来。
伙计，当我第一次把它放在Variac，就像马歇尔那样的野兽，和现在的皮卡之间，没有不想要的反馈-组合是非常理想的。那对我来说就是天堂。当所有这些事情结合在一起时，感觉就像是，好吧，我要为那个糟糕的酒吧疯狂了，我拿到了我的马歇尔和Variac，没有什么能阻止我。
美国专利388117号。吉他插头：将调音插销放在主轴的两侧，可以帮助琴弦保持张力。它还消除了对弦树的需要，也不需要夹紧弦并阻碍弦更换的导轨。
美国专利#4656917乐器支架：一个从吉他后部向下摆动的支架，与您的身体成90度角支撑，让您可以像膝上吉他一样弹奏乐器。
范海伦于2007年开始制造自己的设备，品牌为EVH Gear。他最新推出的沃尔夫冈工作组标准版(Wolfgang WG Standard)于今年春天推出。以范海伦儿子的名字命名的入门级吉他是由极轻和多孔的低音木材制成的，为那些重音和褪色的音乐家提供了完美的共鸣。领口是枫木的，特意采用了最低限度的缎面处理。
范·海伦说，木头越多孔，你就会得到越多的色调。我的吉他没有密封，所以它们能呼吸。树液可以逸出，让木头老化。如果你真的哭了，专业的弗洛伊德·玫瑰桥(Floyd Rose Bridge)会将琴弦锁在两个地方，而不是只锁在一个地方，这样吉他就能保持调子，即使你击中了潜水炸弹--你知道你会尝试的歌曲结尾的高音哀鸣。

Eddie Van Halen , the legendary guitarist and leader of the pioneering metal band Van Halen, passed away on October 6, 2020 at age 65, after battling cancer. Widely considered to be the greatest guitarist of his generation—and maybe of all time—it isn't a stretch to say the rock god influenced every modern player who came after him. Van Halen's wildly inventive innovations, including tapping, or the act of playing the guitar using both left and right hands on the neck, redefined what musicians could do with the instrument—and what rock and roll music could sound like. Van Halen even  patented some of his game-changing techniques.
 Van Halen wrote this piece for Popular Mechanics  in 2015, discussing his patents, rebuilding his guitars and amps, and searching for his signature sound. To honor him, Pop Mech  is reprinting the article in its entirety. May he rest in peace.
  I've always been a tinkerer. It comes from my dad. Growing up, we lived in a house in Pasadena that had no driveway. You used an alley that ran through the middle of the block, behind all the houses, to get to your backyard or the garage. Well, the neighbor behind us had a U-Haul trailer up on car jacks and loaded with cinder block.
 One night my dad came home from a gig at three in the morning. He had a little heat going, he'd had a few drinks, so he says, "This thing is blocking me from getting in again." So he got out of the car and tried to move it. As soon as he lifted the trailer, the jack fell over, and it chopped his finger off.
  This was a problem. Besides the obvious reasons, he played clarinet and saxophone. On a sax, you don't need to seal the hole with your finger. A valve closes over it. But with a clarinet, you have to seal the hole, so he took a saxophone valve cover and adapted it to work on his clarinet.
 Another funny thing was later in his life, when he started losing his teeth. You need your bottom teeth to play a reed instrument. Instead of going to the dentist, he made himself a perfectly shaped prosthesis out of white Teflon that filled the gap where his teeth were missing. He slipped that in when he had to play. Watching him do that kind of stuff instilled a curiosity in me. If something doesn't do what you want it to, there's always a way to fix it.

   My playing style really grew from the fact that I couldn't afford a distortion pedal. I had to try to squeeze those sounds out of my guitar. The first real work I did was in my bedroom. I added pickups, because I didn't like the sound of the originals.
 I couldn't afford a router—I didn't even know what a router was—so I started hammering away with a screwdriver. That didn't work at all. Chunks of wood flew off and there was sawdust flying all over the place. But I was on a mission. I knew what I wanted and I just kept at it until I finally got there.
  Most guitar necks are too round on the back, so I took sandpaper and reshaped the neck to be very flat. I actually refretted a few guitars early on because I wanted to shave the fingerboard down and make the neck even flatter. The flatter it was, the farther I could bend a string without fretting out, or choking the sound when the string hits a fret higher on the neck.
 The other issue, with Fenders, at least, was the clear lacquer they'd put on the neck. When you sweat, your fingers either slide all over the place or get sticky. I couldn't stand that, so when I built my first guitar, I used natural wood. My own sweat and oil would soak in to make it smooth. It took a lot of playing to get it that way but, eventually, it just felt so much better than any synthetic product you could put on there.

   Vibrato bars (also called whammy bars or tremolos) just didn't stay in tune. The problem was the nut—the string guides at the end of the guitar neck. On the first album I used a standard, nonlocking Fender tremolo. The string is angled down from the nut to the tuning pegs, creating tension that, after the string slides back and forth when you use the whammy bar, keeps the string from returning to its original slot. I made my own nut with really smooth indentations—big and round like the bottom of a boat. I put a drop of 3-In-One oil in there, too, so the string would be extra slippery.
 On top of that, instead of winding the string down on the tuning peg, creating an angle and causing that tension, I would wind it up so that, from the nut all the way back to the bridge, the string was level. Otherwise there could be hangups in the nut that would make the guitar go out of tune when you went crazy on the whammy bar.
 The only problem this caused was when you hit an open string, where your fingers aren't holding it down. Without that tension, the string would pop out of the nut slot, so I'd have to remember to put my finger on the far side of the nut to hold things together.
   If it was movable, or turnable, or anything that resembled something that could go up or down, I would mess with it to make the amp run hotter. I opened the amp up and saw this thing. I found out later it was a bias control, which controls the power to the output tubes. I'm poking around, and all of a sudden I touch this huge blue thing and my God, it was like being punched in the chest by Mike Tyson. My whole body flexed stiff, and it must have thrown me five feet. I'd touched a capacitor. I didn't know they held voltage.
 The Marshall amp I brought home from the store where I worked was only good if you turned it all the way up. Any lower and you'd lose the distortion. I needed that, but it was impossible to play anywhere with the volume that loud, so I tried everything, from leaving the thick plastic cover on it to facing it backwards to putting it face down. I'd blow a fuse twice an hour.
  Luckily, I stumbled onto the Variac transformer soon after. I'd bought another Marshall amp, and I had no idea that it was actually a European model. I plugged it in, and I'm waiting for it to warm up and thinking, I got ripped off here, there's no sound coming out! Pissed off, I came back an hour later to give it another shot.
  I'd left the amp on the whole time. I didn't know it was set on 220, so when I turn my guitar on it sounds like a full-blown Marshall, all the way up, except really, really quiet. That was when I realized there was something going on with the voltage. There were these cheesy light dimmers in the house, and I hooked it up to one of those.
 Of course I wired it backwards and shorted out the whole house, so I went down to a place in Pasadena and asked if there was some kind of industrial-size variable transformer that would let me adjust voltage, and they introduced me to the Variac. It's just a huge light dimmer. I plugged it into the amp and controlled the voltage from that. That became my volume knob. I would set the voltage depending on the size of the room we were playing, getting all that feedback at any volume.
  My first real guitar was a Les Paul Goldtop. I was a total Eric Clapton freak, and I saw old pictures of him playing a Les Paul. Except his had humbucking pickups, and mine had the soapbar, P-90 single coils. The first thing I did with that guitar was chisel it out in the back and put a humbucker in. When we were playing gigs, people kept saying, "How is he getting that sound out of single—coil soapbar pickups?" Since my hand was covering the humbucker, they never realized that I'd put it in.
 When my guitar was black and white, I cut out my own pickguard so it would cover the holes from the pickup I'd removed. But when I painted red on top of the black and white, which is how it is now, it didn't look cool with that black pickguard. It covered most of the paint job. I decided just to take the switch and cram it in the middle and put a nonworking pickup in the front because I didn't use it. I wasn't trying to trick anyone. Bottom line is, I didn't know how to hook it back up.
 The last real step for me was adding paraffin wax to my pickup. Pickups can have this really high-end squeal, like the annoying screech of feedback you sometimes hear when someone speaks into a microphone. I thought maybe what was causing that with a guitar was the coil windings vibrating. So what I did—and I have no idea where this idea came from—was buy a hot plate and bricks of paraffin, and borrow a Yuban coffee can from my mom to put the wax in.
  Of course I ruined a lot of pickups, because the plastic frames would melt before I had a chance to yank the pickup out. But finally, when I had a chance to really keep an eye on it, as soon as I saw the pickup start to heat up and shrivel a little bit I'd yank it out.
 Man, the first time I put that in—between the Variac, the beast that Marshall was, and now the pickup not having unwanted feedback—the combination was just ideal. That was heaven to me. When all those things came together, it was like, okay, I'm going crazy with the whammy bar, I got my Marshall with the Variac, there's no stopping me.
  U.S. Patent #388117. Guitar peghead: Placing the tuning pegs on the opposite sides of the headstocks helps the strings hold tension. It also obviates the need for string trees, guides that clamp down on your strings and hinder string replacement.
 U.S. ­Patent #4656917 ­Musical instrument support: A bracket that swings down from the back of the guitar, supporting it at a 90-degree angle from your body and letting you play the instrument like a lap guitar.
    Van Halen started manufacturing his own equipment in 2007 under the brand  EVH Gear. His newest offering, the Wolfgang WG Standard, launched in the spring. Named for Van Halen's son, the entry-level guitar is made from extremely lightweight and porous basswood, providing the perfect resonance for musicians who are heavy on the treble and fade. The neck is maple, with a deliberately minimal satin finish.
 "The more porous the wood, the more tone you're going to get," Van Halen says. "My guitars aren't sealed, so they breathe. The sap can escape, allowing the wood to age." If you get good enough to really wail, the specialty Floyd Rose bridge locks the strings to the body in two places instead of just one, so the guitar stays in tune, even when you hit a dive bomb—the high-pitched whine at the end of the song that you know you're going to try.

思科(Cisco)因抄袭Centripetal Networks的网络安全技术，并迫使该公司退出利润丰厚的政府合同，受到了19亿美元的巨额专利侵权账单的打击。
弗吉尼亚州一家法院周一裁定，这家网络交换机制造商侵犯了四项专利，但由于侵权行为是“故意和恶劣的”，法官将所欠7.56亿美元乘以2.5，总计罚款1889521,362.50美元。法庭称，连同利息，思科将面临一笔金额高达1,903,239,287.50美元的账单，“在判决日一次性到期”。
这还不是全部：法院还对思科未来三年的部分产品征收10%的版税，之后三年征收5%的版税。法官说，前三年的特许权使用费必须至少为1.68亿美元，不超过3亿美元，之后三年的特许权使用费必须在8400万美元到1.5亿美元之间。
尽管金额很大，但远不是毁灭性的，而且对思科来说大约是三个月的利润。这家网络巨头还有大约300亿美元的巨额现金储备，总账单几乎不会吃掉。
至于技术本身，总部位于弗吉尼亚州的Centripetal Networks开发了一种网络保护系统，该系统部分由美国政府资助。它的专利部分解决了速度和可扩展性问题，并允许实时更新和自动化工作流。在思科签署保密协议后，它向思科概述了这项技术。但随后思科在2017年简单地窃取了该功能，并将其整合到自己的产品中。次年，向心力起诉[PDF]。
“思科在这些会议后的一年内发布了具有Centripetal功能的产品，这一事实不仅仅是巧合，”地区法官亨利·摩根(Henry Morgan)在他的判断中说。他指出，思科“不断从Centripetal收集信息，就好像它打算从Centripetal购买技术”，但随后又“挪用了在这些会议上获得的信息，以了解Centripetal的专利功能，并将其嵌入到自己的产品中。”
他还指出，思科阻止其工程师回答有关他们自己的声明和文件的问题，他们在这些声明和文件中称赞Centripetal Networks的工作“解决了以前认为无法解决的问题”。
法官还再次表达了对思科的愤怒，因为思科试图迫使法院使用自己的Webex视频会议系统，而不是法院培训工作人员的Zoom软件：“虽然思科反对在视频/音频平台上审判此案，特别是法院工作人员接受…培训的平台。”这起案件的不同寻常之处在于，它实际上完全是由于新冠肺炎的大流行而举行的。
话虽如此，法官还是对思科的律师们竖起了大拇指，因为他们最终加入了诉讼程序，并帮助进行了审判。这一合作可能为思科节省了一小笔钱，因为法官指出，他决定对制造商造成的“增加的损害”已经“由于其审判律师的专业表现而减轻了”。
就思科而言，它声称其网络安全功能甚至在Centripetal成立之前就已经开发出来了，并否认它破坏了公司赢得政府合同的努力，声称没有人想要Centripetal，因为它的方法“太复杂”。
不过，法官并不买账：“思科的无效证据经常与其不侵权证据相矛盾，而且不承认它在保密协议期间和之后从Centripetal复制的新功能。”
The Register-独立于科技界的新闻和观点。情况发布的一部分
note_en:
Cisco has been hit with a massive $1.9bn patent-infringement bill for copying cybersecurity tech from Centripetal Networks and pushing the company out of lucrative government contracts.
  The network switch maker infringed four patents, a Virginia court decided on Monday, but since the infringement was “willful and egregious,” the judge multiplied the $756m owed by 2.5 to a total fine of $1,889,521,362.50. With interest, Cisco faces a hefty $1,903,239,287.50 bill “payable in a lump sum due on the judgment date,” the court said.
    That’s not all: the court also imposed [ PDF] a royalty of ten per cent of some of Cisco’s products for the next three years, and five per cent for three years after that. That royalty must be at least $168m and no more than $300m for the first three years, and between $84m and $150m for the next three, the judge said.
  Even though the sums are massive, they are far from ruinous, and represent about three months of profit for Cisco. The networking giant also has a massive cash pile of roughly $30bn that the total bill will barely eat into.
  As for the tech itself, Centripetal Networks, based in Virginia, developed a network protection system that was in part funded by the US government. The patented parts of it deal with speed and scalability issues, and allowed for live updates and automated workflows. It outlined the technology to Cisco after the company had signed a non-disclosure agreement. But then Cisco simply stole the functionality and incorporated it into its own products in 2017. Centripetal sued [ PDF] the following year.
  “The fact that Cisco released products with Centripetal’s functionality within a year of these meetings goes beyond mere coincidence,” said District Judge Henry Morgan in his judgment. He noted that Cisco had “continually gathered information from Centripetal as if it intended to buy the technology from Centripetal,” but then “appropriated the information gained in these meetings to learn about Centripetal’s patented functionality and embedded it into its own products.”
    He also noted that Cisco prevented its engineers from answering questions about their own statements and documents in which they praised Centripetal Networks’ work for having “solved problems previously thought unsolvable.”
  The judge also expressed his irritation again with Cisco over its efforts to force the court to  use its own Webex video conferencing system rather than the Zoom software the court had trained its staff on: “While Cisco objected to trying the case on a video/audio platform, and specifically the platform upon which the court’s staff was trained…” The case was unusual in that it was held entirely virtually thanks to the COVID-19 pandemic.
  Having said that, the judge gave Cisco’s lawyers the thumbs up for finally getting on board and helping make the trial work, and that piece of collaboration may have saved Cisco a small fortune as the judge noted that the “enhanced damages” he decided to lay against the manufacturer had been “mitigated by the professional performance of its trial counsel.”
  For its part, Cisco claimed its cybersecurity features had been developed before Centripetal had even been founded, and denied that it had undermined the company’s efforts to win government contracts, claiming that nobody wanted Centripetal because its approach was “too complicated.”
  The judge wasn’t buying it though: “Cisco’s invalidity evidence often contradicted its non-infringement evidence and failed to recognize the new functionality which it copied from Centripetal during and after the Nondisclosure Agreement.”
         The Register - Independent news and views for the tech community. Part of Situation Publishing
title: 思科在窃取Biz的网络安全专利后被勒令交出20亿美元外加版税
title_en: Cisco Ordered To Cough Up $2 Billion Plus Royalties After Ripping Off Biz's Cybersecurity Patents

note_en:
When it comes to covering your home or small office -- which are often the same things these days -- with Wi-Fi, it's hard to beat  mesh networking. Now  Eero, an Amazon company, is introducing Eero for Service Providers. This is an all-new hardware and software offering designed to help internet service providers (ISPs) meet customers' increasing demands for exceptional home Wi-Fi.
 This is not just a bundling of a selection of  Eero Wi-Fi mesh routers with your existing internet service. It also includes remote network management for your ISP and security and privacy management tools for you.
 The bundle starts, of course, with the routers. Besides offering Eero's existing whole-home mesh Wi-Fi systems to customers, ISPs will also get access to the  all-new Eero 6 series. These come with  Wi-Fi 6. This new Wi-Fi technology supports faster speeds and more simultaneously connected devices. Eero claims that this is its fastest Wi-Fi network yet.
  The Eero Pro 6, which is a tri-band router that offers a single band and connects via 2.4GHz, but it also offers two bands for 5GHz to allow more devices to connect at the fastest speeds. It can cover up to 2,000 square feet per router.
   View Now at Amazon
 Eero Pro 6's less expensive brother, the  Eero 6, is a dual-band device that can cover up to 1,500 square feet. Both the Pro 6 and Eero 6 have two Ethernet ports and a USB-C port for charging devices.
   View Now at Amazon
 These new devices also come with a built-in Zigbee smart home hub. This IEEE 802.15.4 personal-area network standard Internet of Things (IoT) hub lets you manage compatible IoT devices on your networks. This way you don't need a separate Zigbee hub.
 The new Eero Secure will give both users and ISPs advanced security and privacy subscription service. This blocks malware, spyware, phishing, and other malicious threats. It also comes with built-in, ad blocking, and content filtering
 For ISPs, Eero Insight builds on Eero's existing Remote Network Management software. This combines monitoring user history to predict and address customer problems before they change from annoyances to real problems. It also includes network monitoring tools such as a network topology viewer, historical speed tests and bandwidth usage, RF diagnostics, alerts, audit logs, outage detection, fleet analysis, and network health.
   For users, all this should mean a more reliable internet connection and that's always good news.
