对大脑活动的研究表明，当狗看到头的后部或前部时，没有什么不同。
狗主人可能会喜欢他们的宠物可爱的小狗眼睛和可爱的毛茸茸的特征，但事实证明，狗的大脑对我们的后脑勺和前脑勺一样兴奋。
研究人员发现，尽管狗进化出了牵动主人心弦的面部表情，但与人类不同的是，狗的大脑区域没有专门对脸做出反应的区域。
匈牙利Eötvös Loránd大学的这项研究的合著者阿提拉·安迪克斯博士说：“令人惊讶的是，狗在阅读情感和辨别面部表情方面做得如此出色，尽管它们的大脑似乎并没有设计成专注于[它们]。”阿提拉·安迪克斯博士是这项研究的合著者，来自匈牙利的Eötvös Loránd大学。
Andics及其同事在“神经科学杂志”(Journal Of NeuroScience)上撰文称，他们扫描了20只家犬的大脑，其中包括拉布拉多犬和边境牧羊犬，并对30名人类进行了扫描，每个人展示了6个序列，每组48个视频，分别是人类或狗的头部的前部或后部。
研究小组发现，狗大脑的特定区域根据所示物种的不同表现出不同的活动，对狗的视频反应更大。然而，在任何地区，当狗被展示为人的脸或狗的脸时，与它的后脑勺相比，没有什么不同。
相比之下，人类大脑的不同区域表现出不同的活动，这取决于展示的是脸部还是后脑勺，脸部通常会产生更强的反应。
这些区域中的一小部分也显示出不同物种之间的差异，总体上表现出对人类的更强反应。
安迪克斯说，进一步的分析表明，狗的大脑主要集中在动物看的是狗还是人，而人类的大脑主要集中在是否有一张脸。
虽然之前的研究表明，狗的大脑中有不同的区域来处理人和狗的面孔，但安迪克斯说，新的结果表明，这些研究可能会注意到对图像中其他差异的反应，比如狗的品种。
安迪克斯说，新的结果表明，狗在交流时并不强烈依赖面孔-但这并不意味着狗完全忽视了面孔。相反，他说，狗的大脑并不是专门设计成专注于脸部的，这可能与动物接受许多身体暗示有关。
伦敦大学学院认知神经科学研究所所长索菲·斯科特教授说，众所周知，人脑中的不同网络处理脸部信息的不同方面。但这项研究表明，狗的大脑工作方式不同。
她说：“狗脸系统只是说‘它是狗还是人’，它并不真的在乎脸。”她指出，这一发现与研究表明狗和人类都有特定的大脑区域参与处理声音形成了鲜明对比。
斯科特补充说，这一结果表明，与其他信息相比，狗对脸的依赖程度可能更低。她说：“狗知道他们的朋友是谁以及他们在做什么的主要方式之一就是他们的气味。”
但来自埃默里大学的人类视觉皮层专家丹尼尔·迪尔克斯博士表示，这项研究并不能最终证明狗的大脑中没有脸部特异的区域。他补充说：“狗的[大脑]区域(只对狗的图像做出反应)的发现很耐人寻味，但只有50%的被测试狗显示出这样的区域。”“理解为什么一半的狗有这样的皮层，而另一半没有，这一点很重要。”
note_en:
Study of brain activity shows no difference when dogs see back or front of a head
    Dog owners might love their pet’s endearing puppy dog eyes and cute furry features, but it turns out the doggy brain is just as excited by the back of our heads as the front.
 For despite having  evolved facial expressions that tug on the heartstrings of owners, researchers have found that unlike humans, dogs do not have brain regions that respond specifically to faces.
  “It’s amazing dogs do so well when it comes to reading emotions and identify from faces, despite the fact that they seem not to have a brain designed for having a focus on [them],” said Dr Attila Andics, co-author of the study from Eötvös Loránd University, Hungary.
 Writing in the Journal of Neuroscience, Andics and colleagues report how they scanned the brains of 20 family dogs, including labradors and border collies, and 30 humans with each shown six sequences of 48 videos of either the front or the back of a human or dog head.
 The team found particular regions of the dog’s brain showed differing activity depending on the species shown, with a greater response to dog videos. However, there was no difference in any region when dogs were shown a human or dog face compared with the back of its head.
 By contrast, regions of the human brain showed different activity depending whether a face or the back of a head was shown, with faces generally generating a stronger response.
 A small subset of these regions also showed a difference between species, in general showing a stronger response to humans.
 Andics said the further analysis showed the dog brain was primarily focused on whether the animal was looking at a dog or a human, whereas the human brain was mainly focused on whether there was a face.
 While previous work has suggested that dogs have separate areas of the brain for processing human and dog faces, Andics said the new results suggest these studies might be picking up on responses to other differences in the images, such as the breed of dog.
 Andics said the new results suggested dogs did not rely strongly on faces when it comes to communication – but that did not mean dogs completely ignored them. Rather, he said, dog brains were not designed to specifically focus on faces, something that might be linked to the animals taking in many body cues.
 Prof Sophie Scott, director of the Institute for Cognitive  Neuroscience at University College London, said it was known that different networks in the human brain processes different aspects of information in faces. But the study suggests the canine brain works differently.
 “The dog face system just goes ‘it’s a dog or a human’ and it doesn’t really care about the faces,” she said, noting the findings contrast to research showing both dogs and humans have particular brain regions involved in processing voices.
 The results, Scott added, suggests dogs may be rely less on faces than other information. “One of the main ways dogs know who their friends are and how they are doing is their smell,” she said.
 But Dr Daniel Dilks, an expert in the human visual cortex from Emory University, said the study did not conclusively prove there was no face-specific brain region in dogs. “The finding of a [brain] region in dogs [that only responds to images of dogs] is intriguing, but only 50% of the dogs tested showed such a region,” he added. “It will be important to understand why half of the dogs exhibit such a cortex, while the other half does not.”
title: 狗的“大脑不是硬连线的”，不能对人的脸做出反应
title_en: Dogs' Brains 'Not Hardwired' To Respond To Human Faces

note:
周二，诺贝尔物理学奖被授予三位天体物理学家，以表彰他们在世界乃至宇宙中所做的无与伦比的工作。他们是英国人罗杰·彭罗斯、德国人莱因哈德·根泽尔和美国人安德里亚·盖兹。他们因在通向永恒之门的黑洞方面的工作而受到认可，黑洞是一种吞噬光线的巨大物体，以及永远落在他们无情的嘴里的其他一切东西。
彭罗斯博士是牛津大学(Oxford University)的数学家，他获得了大约110万美元奖金的一半，以证明如果阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦(Albert Einstein)的引力理论(即广义相对论)是正确的，那么黑洞肯定存在。
Genzel博士和Ghez博士对我们银河系中心的这只黑暗怪物进行了长达数十年的不懈研究，收集证据证明它是一个超大质量黑洞。
盖兹博士是继1903年玛丽·居里(Marie Curie)、1963年玛丽亚·戈珀特·梅耶尔(Maria Goeppert Mayer)和2018年唐娜·斯特里克兰(Donna Strickland)之后，第四位获得诺贝尔物理学奖的女性。
诺贝尔大会在斯德哥尔摩的瑞典皇家科学院宣布了该奖项。
黑洞是1915年11月首次宣布的爱因斯坦广义相对论中最早也是最极端的预测之一。该理论解释了我们称之为重力的力，因为物体试图沿着一条直线穿过一个几何形状被物质和能量扭曲的宇宙。因此，行星和光束都沿着弯曲的路径运动，就像绕着轮盘赌的球一样。
几个月后，当德国天文学家卡尔·施瓦茨希尔德(Karl Schwarzschild)指出，这些方程包含了世界末日的预言时，爱因斯坦大吃一惊：实际上，在太小的空间里塞进太多的物质和能量，会导致时空塌缩成一个叫做奇点的无限密度的点。在那个地方-如果你可以称之为一个地方-爱因斯坦方程式或任何其他物理定律都没有意义。
爱因斯坦在数学上无可挑剔，但他认为在现实生活中，大自然会找到一种方法来避免这样的灾难。
然而，在爱因斯坦去世十年后的1965年，彭罗斯博士关上了爱因斯坦希望的大门。
彭罗斯博士1931年出生于一个知识分子家庭，是牛津大学的教授。彭罗斯博士在最近的一次采访中回忆说，当他年轻的时候，一家人在乡下散步，他们会在头脑中下棋，在没有实体棋盘的情况下跟踪各种走法。
周一，哈维·J·奥尔特博士、迈克尔·霍顿博士和查尔斯·M·赖斯博士因发现丙型肝炎病毒而获奖。
周二，罗杰·彭罗斯(Roger Penrose)、莱因哈德·根泽尔(Reinhard Genzel)和安德里亚·盖兹(Andrea Ghez)因他们的发现改善了人们对宇宙的理解而获奖，其中包括对黑洞的研究。
“我的工作是跑，”他说，“我会从一个兄弟那里学到动作，然后跑到我父亲那里。”我只是通过来回跑来锻炼身体。“。
作为一名才华横溢的数学家，他发明了一种描述时空的新方法，称为彭罗斯图，该图绕过了广义相对论的大部分数学复杂性。
他的图表现在是宇宙学的通用语。他证明，如果在一个太小的地方积累了太多的质量，塌缩成黑洞是不可避免的。在黑洞的边界，称为视界，你必须比光速更快-公认的宇宙速度极限-才能逃脱。这样你就再也逃不掉了。在边界内，时间和空间会互换角色，所以所有方向都会向下通向中心，在那里密度变得无限大，我们所知道的物理定律将被打破。
他证明黑洞将成为通往时间尽头、宇宙尽头的门户。
他还以发现Penrose瓷砖而闻名，这是一种在不重复图案的情况下铺设无限地板的方法。他还在“皇帝的新思维：关于计算机、思维和物理定律”等书中发表了对人工智能和意识起源的颠覆传统的观点。
在欢呼这一消息的同时，一些天文学家和物理学家哀叹斯蒂芬·霍金(Stephen Hawking)的缺席。霍金是剑桥大学(Cambridge University)的宇宙学家，可以说是世界上领先的黑洞理论家，直到2018年去世，使他没有资格获得诺贝尔奖。
彭罗斯博士做出突破性计算后不久，霍金和彭罗斯博士用同样的方法合作证明，如果广义相对论是正确的，宇宙肯定也有了一个开端--一个相当重大的发现。
加州理工大学物理学家约翰·普雷斯基尔(John Preskill)在推特上庆祝了盖兹博士和其他科学家的成就。但他补充说，这一时刻令人心酸。
他说：“我在想，斯蒂芬·霍金分享广义相对论进步奖会有多高兴。”
今天，天文学家们一致认为，宇宙中散布着这样的黑暗怪物，包括潜伏在大多数星系中心的野兽，这些星系的质量是太阳的数百万倍和数十亿倍。他们甚至在大约5500万光年之外的星系中拍摄了一张照片。
但是离我们更近的地方，在我们银河系的中心，距离我们26000光年的地方，有一个微弱的射电噪声源，叫做人马座A*。1971年，马丁·里斯(Martin Rees)和唐纳德·林登-贝尔(Donald Lynden-Bell)提出它是一个超大质量黑洞。
根泽尔博士和盖兹博士以及他们的团队独立工作，在过去的几十年里，他们一直在智利和夏威夷用望远镜追踪围绕银河系中心旋转的恒星和尘埃云，试图看看那个黑暗的尘埃区域是否真的藏匿着黑洞。
盖兹博士于1965年6月16日出生于纽约。她是加州大学洛杉矶分校(University of California，Los Angeles)的教授，也是儿童读物“你可以成为一名女性天文学家”(You Can Be A Women Astronomer)的作者之一。周二，她指出自己是第四位获得物理学奖的女性，她说她希望能激励年轻女性。
“这是一个有很多乐趣的领域，如果你对科学充满热情，就会有很多事情可以做，”她说。
根泽尔博士是德国加兴马克斯·普朗克地外物理研究所所长，也是加州大学伯克利分校的教授。
他在德国的弗莱堡长大，弗莱堡是黑森林中的一个小城市。年轻时，他是德国最好的标枪运动员之一，甚至为1972年慕尼黑奥运会与国家队一起训练。
根泽尔博士和盖兹博士还因他们的工作分享了其他荣誉，包括2012年的克拉福德奖(Crafoord Award)，通常被称为天文学诺贝尔奖。
多年来，他们的观测越来越接近于得出这样的结论：银河系中心的任何东西都是黑暗的，质量必须相当于400万个太阳，才能施加足够的引力来控制围绕它旋转的恒星和气体。
根泽尔博士称之为S2，盖兹博士称之为S0-2的其中一颗恒星是一颗年轻的蓝色恒星，它沿着非常拉长的轨道运行，每隔16年就会经过距离假定的黑洞口仅110亿英里(约合17光小时)的范围内。
根据爱因斯坦的说法，在这些令人担忧的过程中，这颗以每秒5000英里的速度绕着蛋形轨道旋转的恒星应该会体验到宇宙的完全陌生。上一次发生这种情况是在2018年夏天，两支球队都在关注这位明星的偏差或惊喜。
为了进行这项实验，天文学家需要高精度地了解恒星的轨道，这反过来需要用地球上最强大的望远镜进行数十年的观测。
“你需要20年的数据才能在这张桌子上占有一席之地，”盖兹博士说，他在1995年加入了这场竞争。
2018年秋天，根泽尔博士宣布，他们每隔45分钟左右就探测到围绕银河系中心旋转的气体云，速度为光速的30%。根泽尔博士说，这些云离疑似黑洞太近了，根据爱因斯坦的经典物理学，如果它们再近一点，它们就会掉进去。
Genzel博士的研究小组在2018年的“天文学与天体物理学”杂志上写道，这些结果提供了“强有力的支持”，即射手座的黑暗物体“确实是一个大质量的黑洞”。
瑞典科学院在公告中说：“他们的开创性工作为我们提供了银河系中心存在超大质量黑洞的最令人信服的证据。”
物理学家说，知道黑洞的存在只会提醒我们，我们并不了解黑洞内部发生了什么，我们也没有真正了解引力。
在1998年出版的自传中，广义相对论的领导者之一、普林斯顿大学(Princeton)和德克萨斯大学奥斯汀分校(University Of Texas At Austin)教授约翰·惠勒(John Wheeler)在他的自传中说，黑洞“告诉我们，空间可以像一张纸一样压缩成一个无限小的点，时间可以像熄灭的火焰一样熄灭，而我们认为‘神圣’、一成不变的物理定律绝不是这样。”惠勒是广义相对论的领军人物之一，曾在普林斯顿大学和得克萨斯大学奥斯汀分校(University Of Texas At Austin)担任教授。
大多数物理学家认为，爱因斯坦的广义相对论将需要修改，以应对诸如大爆炸或黑洞中发生的任何极端情况。
盖兹博士在几年前的一次采访中说，“我们已经知道爱因斯坦的引力理论已经站不住脚了。”“还有什么地方比超大质量黑洞更适合寻找其中的差异呢？”
去年，宇宙学家詹姆斯·皮布尔斯(James Peeble)与两位天文学家米歇尔·马约尔(Michel Mayor)和迪迪埃·奎洛兹(Didier Queloz)分享了该奖项，诺贝尔奖评委称他们的工作“改变了我们对宇宙的看法”。
2017年，委员会表彰Rainer Weiss、Kip Thorne和Barry Barish发现了来自黑洞的引力波。
现供职于卡夫利基金会(Kavli Foundation)的宇宙学家迈克尔·特纳(Michael Turner)说，“这些天来，天体物理学似乎拥有诺贝尔物理学奖。”他补充说，“鉴于我们对宇宙的所有了解，这是理所当然的。”
周一，哈维·J·奥尔特、迈克尔·霍顿和查尔斯·M·赖斯因发现丙型肝炎病毒而获奖。诺贝尔委员会说，这三位科学家“使血液测试和新药成为可能，拯救了数百万人的生命。”
诺贝尔化学奖将于周三在瑞典揭晓。阅读去年的获奖者约翰·B·古德足够、M·斯坦利·惠廷汉姆和吉野明的故事。
诺贝尔文学奖将于周四在瑞典揭晓。2018年和2019年的奖项都是在2018年的奖项推迟后于去年宣布的。这发生在一名学院成员的丈夫被指控犯有强奸罪并最终被判有罪之后-这场危机导致几名董事会成员离职，需要瑞典国王的干预。
诺贝尔和平奖将于周五在挪威揭晓。了解一下去年的获胜者，埃塞俄比亚总理阿比·艾哈迈德(Abiy Ahmed)。
诺贝尔经济学奖将于下周一在瑞典公布。阅读去年的获奖者阿比吉特·班纳吉、埃斯特·迪弗洛和迈克尔·克雷默的故事。
note_en:
The Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to three astrophysicists Tuesday for work that was literally out of the world, and indeed the universe. They are Roger Penrose, an Englishman, Reinhard Genzel, a German, and Andrea Ghez, an American. They were recognized for their work on the gateways to eternity known as black holes, massive objects that swallow light and everything else forever that falls in their unsparing maws.
 Dr. Penrose, a mathematician at Oxford University, was awarded half of the approximately $1.1 million prize for proving that black holes must exist if  Albert Einstein’s theory of gravity, known as general relativity, is right.
 The second half was split between Dr. Genzel and Dr. Ghez for their relentless and decades long investigation of the dark monster here in the center of our own galaxy, gathering evidence to convict it of being a supermassive black hole.
 Dr. Ghez is only the fourth woman to win the Nobel Prize in Physics, following  Marie Curie in 1903,  Maria Goeppert Mayer in 1963 and  Donna Strickland in 2018.
 The Nobel Assembly announced the prize at the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences in Stockholm.
  Black holes were one of the first and most extreme predictions of Einstein’s General Theory of Relativity, first announced in November 1915. The theory explains the force we call gravity, as objects try to follow a straight line through a universe whose geometry is warped by matter and energy. As a result, planets as well as light beams follow curving paths, like balls going around a roulette wheel.
 Einstein was taken aback a few months later when Karl Schwarzschild, a German astronomer, pointed out that the equations contained an apocalyptic prediction: In effect, cramming too much matter and energy inside too small a space would cause space-time to collapse into a point of infinite density called a singularity. In that place — if you could call it a place — neither Einstein’s equations nor any other physical law made sense.
 Einstein could not fault the math, but he figured that in real life, nature would find a way to avoid such a calamity.
 In 1965, however, a decade after Einstein’s death, Dr. Penrose slammed the door on Einstein’s hopes.
  Born in 1931 into an intellectual family, Dr. Penrose is a professor at the University of Oxford. Dr. Penrose recalled in an interview recently that when he was young and the family took walks in the country, they would play chess in their heads, keeping track of various moves without a physical board.
 Drs. Harvey J. Alter, Michael Houghton and Charles M. Rice on Monday received the prize for their  discovery of the hepatitis C virus.
 Roger Penrose, Reinhard Genzel and Andrea Ghez received the prize on Tuesday for their discoveries that have  improved understanding of the universe, including work on black holes.
 “My job was the runner,” he said, “I would take the moves from one brother and race up to my father. And I just got exercise by running back and forth.”
 A talented mathematician, he invented a new way of portraying space-time, called a Penrose diagram, which bypassed most of the mathematical complexities of general relativity.
 His diagrams are now the lingua franca of cosmology. He proved that if too much mass accumulated in too small a place, collapse into a black hole was inevitable. At the boundary of a black hole, called the event horizon, you would have to go faster than the speed of light — the acknowledged cosmic speed limit — to get away. So you could never escape. Inside the boundary, time and space would switch roles and so all directions would lead downward, to the center, where the density became infinite and the laws of physics, as we knew them, would break down.
 He showed that the black hole would become a gateway to the end of time, the end of the universe.
 He is also famous for discovering Penrose tiles, a way of tiling an infinite floor without ever repeating the pattern. He has also published iconoclastic views of artificial intelligence and the origins of consciousness in books like “The Emperor’s New Mind:   Concerning Computers, Minds and the Laws of Physics .”
 As they hailed the news, some astronomers and physicists lamented the absence of  Stephen Hawking, the Cambridge University cosmologist who was arguably the world’s leading black hole theorist until he died in 2018, making him ineligible for the Nobel.
 Shortly after Dr. Penrose made his breakthrough calculations, Dr. Hawking and Dr. Penrose collaborated using the same methods to prove that if general relativity was right, the universe must also have had a beginning — a fairly big discovery.
 John Preskill, a Caltech physicist, celebrated the accomplishment of Dr. Ghez and the other scientists  in a tweet. But he added that the moment was poignant.
 “I’m thinking of how much Stephen Hawking would have enjoyed sharing a Prize for advances in General Relativity,” he said.
  Today, astronomers agree that the universe is speckled with such dark monsters, including beasts lurking in the hearts of most galaxies that are millions and billions of times as massive as the sun. They’ve  even taken a picture of one in a galaxy some 55 million light-years away.
 But closer to home, at the center of our Milky Way galaxy, 26,000 light-years from here, there is a faint source of radio noise called Sagittarius A*. In 1971 Martin Rees and Donald Lynden-Bell suggested that it was a  supermassive black hole.
 Working independently, Dr. Genzel and Dr. Ghez, and their teams,  have spent the last decades tracking stars and dust clouds whizzing around the center of our galaxy with telescopes in Chile and Hawaii, trying to see if that dark dusty realm does indeed harbor a black hole.
 Dr. Ghez was born in New York on June 16, 1965. She is a professor at the University of California, Los Angeles and one of the authors of the children’s book  “You Can Be a Woman Astronomer.” Noting on Tuesday that she was only the fourth woman to win the physics prize, she said that she hoped to inspire young women.
 “It’s a field that has so many pleasures, and if you’re passionate about the science, there is so much that can be done,” she said.
 Dr. Genzel is a director at the Max Planck Institute for Extraterrestrial Physics in Garching, Germany, and a professor at the University of California, Berkeley.
 He grew up in Freiburg, Germany, a small city in the Black Forest. As a young man, he was one of the best javelin throwers in Germany, even training with the national team for the 1972 Munich Olympics.
 Dr. Genzel and Dr. Ghez have shared other honors for their work, including the Crafoord Prize in 2012, often referred to as the astronomy Nobel.
 Over the years, their observations have crept closer to the conclusion that whatever is at the galactic center is dark and must have a mass equivalent to four million suns, in order to exert enough gravitational pull to keep the stars and gas that circle it in check.
 One of the stars, which Dr. Genzel calls S2 and Dr. Ghez calls S0-2, is a young blue star that follows a very elongated orbit and passes within just 11 billion miles, or 17 light-hours, of the mouth of the putative black hole every 16 years.
 During these fraught passages, the star, yanked around an egg-shaped orbit at speeds of up to 5,000 miles per second, should experience the full strangeness of the universe, according to Einstein. That last happened in the summer of 2018, with both teams watching for deviation or surprise from the star.
 To conduct that experiment, astronomers needed to know the star’s orbit to a high precision, which in turn required decades of observations with the most powerful telescopes on Earth.
 “You need 20 years of data just to get a seat at this table,” said Dr. Ghez, who joined the fray in 1995.
 In fall 2018, Dr. Genzel announced that they had detected the gas clouds circling the center of the galaxy every 45 minutes or so at 30 percent the speed of light. Those clouds are so close to the suspected black hole that if they were any closer, they would fall in, according to classical Einsteinian physics, Dr. Genzel said.
 The results provide “strong support” that the dark thing in Sagittarius “is indeed a massive black hole,” Dr. Genzel’s group wrote in the journal  Astronomy & Astrophysics in 2018.
 “Their pioneering work has given us the most convincing evidence yet of a supermassive black hole at the centre of the Milky Way,” the Swedish Academy of Sciences said in its announcement.
  Knowing that black holes exist, physicists say, only reminds us that we don’t understand what goes on inside them and that we don’t really understand gravity.
 The black hole “teaches us that space can be crumpled like a piece of paper into an infinitesimal dot, that time can be extinguished like a blown-out flame, and that the laws of physics that we regard as ‘sacred,’ as immutable, are anything but,” said John Wheeler, one of the leaders of general relativity as a professor at Princeton and the University of Texas at Austin, in  his 1998 autobiography.
 Most physicists believe that Einstein’s theory of general relativity will need to be modified to cope with extreme situations such as the Big Bang or whatever does happen in black holes.
 “We already know Einstein’s theory of gravity is fraying around the edges,” Dr. Ghez said in an interview a couple of years ago. “What better places to look for discrepancies in it than a supermassive black hole?”
  Last year, the cosmologist James Peebles split the prize with two astronomers, Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz, for  work the Nobel judges said “transformed our ideas about the cosmos.”
 And in 2017 the committee honored Rainer Weiss, Kip Thorne and Barry Barish for the  discovery of gravitational waves from black holes.
 “Astrophysics seems to own the Nobel Physics Prize these days,” said Michael Turner, a cosmologist now at the Kavli Foundation, adding ”and rightly so with all that we are learning about the Universe.”
  Harvey J. Alter, Michael Houghton and Charles M. Rice on Monday received the prize for their  discovery of the hepatitis C virus. The Nobel committee said the three scientists had “made possible blood tests and new medicines that have saved millions of lives.”
  The Nobel Prize in Chemistry will be announced on Wednesday in Sweden. Read about last year’s winners,  John B. Goodenough, M. Stanley Whittingham and Akira Yoshino.
 The Nobel Prizes in Literature will be announced on Thursday in Sweden. The prizes for  both 2018 and 2019 were announced last year after a postponement of the 2018 prize. That occurred after  the husband of an academy member was accused, and ultimately convicted, of rape — a crisis that led to the departure of several board members and required the intervention of the King of Sweden.
 The Nobel Peace Prize will be announced on Friday in Norway. Read about last year’s winner,  Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed of Ethiopia.
 The Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Science will be announced Monday next week in Sweden. Read about  last year’s winners, Abhijit Banerjee, Esther Duflo and Michael Kremer.
title: 诺贝尔物理学奖授予3名科学家，表彰他们在黑洞方面的工作
title_en: Nobel Prize In Physics Awarded To 3 Scientists For Work On Black Holes
